azure.name: Virtual Network (VNet)
azure.description: Regional isolated network boundary for Azure resources. Subnets span all Availability Zones within the region, so resources can move zones without re-IP.
azure.keyDetail: Regional scope. Up to 1,000 VNets per subscription per region. Supports multiple address spaces per VNet.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  VNet["VNet\n(Regional)"] --> S1[Subnet 1]
  VNet --> S2[Subnet 2]
  style VNet fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
aws.description: Regional isolated network boundary for AWS resources. Subnets are tied to individual Availability Zones, giving granular fault-domain isolation.
aws.keyDetail: Regional scope. Default limit of 5 VPCs per region (soft limit, increasable). Up to 5 CIDR blocks per VPC by default (max 50, raisable).
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  VPC["VPC\n(Regional)"] --> S1["Subnet 1\n(AZ-a)"]
  VPC --> S2["Subnet 2\n(AZ-b)"]
  style VPC fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
gcp.description: Global network boundary that spans all GCP regions. Subnets are regional but the VPC itself is a single global object, fundamentally different from Azure and AWS.
gcp.keyDetail: Global scope. Default limit of 15 VPCs per project (increasable). Up to 500 subnets per VPC.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  VPC["VPC\n(Global)"] --> S1["Subnet\nus-east1"]
  VPC --> S2["Subnet\neu-west1"]
  style VPC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: GCP VPC is global by default, while Azure VNet and AWS VPC are regional. Azure subnets span all AZs in a region, AWS subnets are pinned to a single AZ, and GCP subnets are regional within a global VPC. This fundamentally changes multi-region design: GCP needs one VPC for all regions, whereas Azure/AWS need a VNet/VPC per region.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's global VPC is both a significant architectural difference — simpler for multi-region but you lose the blast-radius isolation that separate regional VNets/VPCs give you. In practice, many organisations on GCP use multiple VPCs for segmentation despite the global model.

azure.name: Subnet
azure.description: A subdivision of a VNet address space. Subnets span all Availability Zones in the region, so VMs can be placed in any zone without changing subnet or IP.
azure.keyDetail: Regional scope (spans all AZs). Up to 3,000 subnets per VNet. Azure reserves 5 IPs per subnet for platform use.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  Sub["Subnet\n(Regional)"] --> AZ1["AZ 1"]
  Sub --> AZ2["AZ 2"]
  Sub --> AZ3["AZ 3"]
  style Sub fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Subnet
aws.description: A subdivision of a VPC tied to a specific Availability Zone. Each subnet exists in exactly one AZ, requiring explicit multi-AZ subnet design for HA.
aws.keyDetail: Zonal scope (one AZ per subnet). Up to 200 subnets per VPC. AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  RT["Route Table"] --> Sub["Subnet\n(Single AZ)"]
  Sub --> AZ["AZ-a"]
  style Sub fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Subnet (Subnetwork)
gcp.description: A regional IP range within a global VPC. Subnets span all zones in the region, similar to Azure. Can use auto-mode (auto-creates one subnet per region) or custom mode.
gcp.keyDetail: Regional scope (spans all zones). Up to 500 subnets per VPC (hard system limit, not raisable). GCP reserves 4 IPs per subnet.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Sub["Subnet\n(Regional)"] --> Auto["Auto-expand\nIP range"]
  style Sub fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS subnets are zonal — you must create separate subnets per AZ for HA, which multiplies subnet count significantly. Azure and GCP subnets are regional, spanning all zones. GCP's auto-mode VPC pre-creates subnets in every region, while Azure and AWS require explicit creation.
features.practitionerNote: AWS's zonal subnet model means a simple 3-tier app across 3 AZs needs 9 subnets minimum. The same design in Azure or GCP needs only 3. This compounds fast in large environments and is a key difference for engineers moving between clouds.

azure.name: Public IP Address
azure.description: A dedicated public IPv4 or IPv6 address resource that can be static or dynamic. Standard SKU is zone-redundant and required for many production workloads (Basic SKU retired September 30, 2025).
azure.keyDetail: Standard SKU: ~$0.005/hr (~$3.72/mo) whether attached or not. Default quota 100-250 per region. Supports IPv6.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  PIP["Public IP\n(SKU: Std)"] --> VM["VM NIC"]
  style PIP fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Elastic IP (EIP)
aws.description: A static public IPv4 address that can be remapped between instances for failover. Since 2024, AWS charges for all public IPv4 addresses whether in use or idle.
aws.keyDetail: ~$0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo) per EIP regardless of attachment. Default limit of 5 EIPs per region. No Elastic IP equivalent for IPv6.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  EIP["Elastic IP\n(Regional)"] --> ENI["ENI"]
  style EIP fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: External IP Address
gcp.description: Static or ephemeral public IP address for VM instances and load balancers. Static IPs are reserved explicitly; ephemeral IPs are auto-assigned at boot and released on stop.
gcp.keyDetail: In-use: ~$0.004/hr (~$2.88/mo). Unused/reserved: ~$0.01/hr (~$7.20/mo) — a significant penalty for idle IPs. Default ~8 static IPs per region (increasable via quota request).
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  IP["External IP\n(Ephemeral/Static)"] --> VM["VM"]
  style IP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three now charge for public IPv4 whether used or not, but GCP charges 2x more for unused/reserved IPs. AWS has no native Elastic IP for IPv6. Azure's Standard SKU is zone-redundant by default, while AWS EIPs are zonal. GCP ephemeral IPs change on every VM restart, which requires automation to update DNS or dependent configs.
features.practitionerNote: The 2024 AWS public IPv4 charging change was significant — it affected every account with public IPv4 addresses — $0.005/hr applies to all public IPv4 addresses, whether in-use or idle. GCP's higher idle reservation cost means you should aggressively clean up reserved-but-unattached IPs. Review each provider's current pricing page before estimating public IP costs.

azure.name: Network Interface (NIC)
azure.description: A separate ARM resource representing a network connection for a VM. NICs are independent objects that can be pre-created, moved between VMs (when stopped), and configured independently.
azure.keyDetail: 1-8+ NICs per VM depending on SKU (up to 20 on largest M-series). Each NIC supports 1 primary + up to 249 secondary private IPs.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  NIC["NIC"] --> IP1["IP Config 1"]
  NIC --> IP2["IP Config 2"]
  style NIC fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Elastic Network Interface (ENI)
aws.description: A virtual network card attached to an EC2 instance. ENIs are independent resources that can be detached and reattached between instances in the same AZ.
aws.keyDetail: Up to 24 ENIs per instance (high-end types). Up to 64 IPv4 addresses per ENI on large instances. ENI count varies dramatically by instance type.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  ENI["ENI\n(Portable)"] --> VM1["Instance A"]
  ENI -.-> VM2["Instance B"]
  style ENI fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Network Interface
gcp.description: A network connection for a Compute Engine VM. Unlike Azure/AWS, GCP NICs are not independent resources — they are defined as part of the VM configuration and cannot be detached.
gcp.keyDetail: NICs per VM varies by machine type (typically 2-8, based on vCPU count). Each NIC supports 1 primary + up to 150 alias IP ranges. NICs cannot be moved between VMs.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  VM["VM"] --> NIC["NIC\n(Non-portable)"]
  style NIC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure and AWS treat NICs/ENIs as independent, portable resources that can be moved between VMs. GCP NICs are bound to the VM lifecycle — you cannot detach and reattach them. AWS offers the highest NIC count on large instances (24), Azure varies widely by SKU, and GCP is a flat 8 for most types.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's non-portable NIC model requires different NVA failover patterns — you need floating IPs or internal load balancers instead of NIC migration. Azure supports up to 249 secondary IPs per NIC, which is relevant for container networking scenarios that assign pod IPs directly to the NIC.

azure.name: Network Security Group (NSG)
azure.description: Stateful firewall rules applied at the subnet or NIC level. Supports both allow and deny rules with priority-based evaluation (lower number = higher priority). No separate stateless NACL layer.
azure.keyDetail: Supports explicit deny rules. Priority-based evaluation (100-4096). Can be applied to both subnets and NICs simultaneously for layered security.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Allow 100] --> D[Deny 200] --> X[Default Deny]
  style A fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Security Group
aws.description: Stateful firewall rules applied at the instance/ENI level. Only supports allow rules — there is no explicit deny. All rules are evaluated together (no priority ordering). AWS also has NACLs as a stateless subnet-level layer.
aws.keyDetail: Allow-only (no deny rules). Orderless evaluation. Paired with Network ACLs (NACLs) at subnet level which are stateless and support deny.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  A1[Allow] --> A2[Allow] --> X[Implicit Deny]
  style X fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VPC Firewall Rules
gcp.description: Stateful firewall rules applied at the VPC level, targeted by network tags or service accounts. Supports both allow and deny rules with priority-based evaluation, similar to Azure NSGs.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports deny rules. Priority-based (0-65535, lower = higher). Applied via tags/service accounts — not directly to subnets or NICs.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Priority 1000] --> B[Priority 2000] --> X[Default Deny]
  style A fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure NSGs and GCP Firewall Rules both support explicit deny with priority ordering. AWS Security Groups are allow-only with no priority — you need NACLs for deny rules, which provides a separate stateless filtering layer. GCP's tag-based targeting is more flexible than Azure's subnet/NIC attachment model but requires disciplined tag management.
features.practitionerNote: A key difference when moving from Azure to AWS: you cannot deny specific traffic in a Security Group. You must use NACLs (which are stateless and complex to manage at scale) or a Network Firewall. Coming from AWS to Azure, the absence of NACLs is a common adjustment — but NSGs support deny rules natively, so Azure does not need a separate stateless filtering layer.

azure.name: VNet Peering
azure.description: Non-transitive, low-latency connection between two VNets using Azure backbone. Supports cross-region (global) peering and gateway transit to share VPN/ExpressRoute gateways with peered VNets.
azure.keyDetail: Up to 500 peerings per VNet (raisable to 1,000 with Azure Virtual Network Manager). Non-transitive. Supports gateway transit. Routes auto-propagate. No overlapping CIDR allowed.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  V1["VNet A"] -- "Non-transitive" --- V2["VNet B"]
  style V1 fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Peering
aws.description: Non-transitive connection between two VPCs, including cross-region and cross-account. Requires manual route table updates in each VPC to enable traffic flow over the peering.
aws.keyDetail: Up to 50 peerings per VPC by default (125 max with quota increase). Non-transitive. Requires manual route updates. No gateway transit equivalent.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  V1["VPC A"] -- "Non-transitive" --- V2["VPC B"]
  style V1 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VPC Network Peering
gcp.description: Non-transitive connection between two VPC networks. Because GCP VPCs are global, a single peering connects all regions in both VPCs. Routes are automatically exchanged.
gcp.keyDetail: Up to 25 peerings per VPC (quota increase available via Cloud Console request). Non-transitive. Automatic route exchange. Lowest peering limit of the three.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  V1["VPC A\n(Global)"] -- "25 peer limit" --- V2["VPC B"]
  style V1 fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three are non-transitive. Azure has the highest peering limit (500), followed by AWS (125), then GCP (25). Azure auto-propagates routes and offers gateway transit; AWS peering uses explicit route table entries and does not support gateway transit — Transit Gateway serves this role instead. GCP's global VPC means one peering covers all regions, unlike Azure/AWS where cross-region peering is a separate configuration.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's default 25-peering quota (increasable via support request) is still a practical constraint for large enterprises — enterprises typically move to Shared VPC or NCC sooner than they would adopt managed WAN solutions on Azure or AWS. Azure's gateway transit is a differentiating feature: spokes to use the hub's ExpressRoute without deploying their own gateway.

azure.name: Private Subnet (defaultOutboundAccess=false)
azure.description: As of March 31, 2026, new Azure VNets/subnets are private by default — no implicit outbound internet access. You must explicitly configure NAT Gateway, Standard LB outbound rules, public IP, or NVA routing for internet egress.
azure.keyDetail: Default outbound access retired for new VNets (March 31, 2026). Existing VNets retain legacy behavior. This was a breaking change that required migration planning for existing workloads.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  Sub["Subnet"] -->|"No default\noutbound"| X["Blocked"]
  Sub -->|"Explicit\nNAT GW"| Out["Internet"]
  style Sub fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Private Subnet (default behavior)
aws.description: AWS has always distinguished public and private subnets by route table configuration. Private subnets have no default internet route — you must add a NAT Gateway or NAT instance for outbound access.
aws.keyDetail: AWS never had implicit outbound for private subnets — explicit NAT Gateway or IGW route required from day one.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  Sub["Private\nSubnet"] -->|"No IGW\nroute"| X["Blocked"]
  Sub -->|"NAT GW"| Out["Internet"]
  style Sub fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VM without External IP
gcp.description: GCP VMs without an external IP have no outbound internet access unless Cloud NAT is configured. The default VPC includes an internet gateway route, but without an external IP or Cloud NAT, VMs cannot reach the internet.
gcp.keyDetail: Default VPC has internet route, but VMs need an external IP or Cloud NAT to use it. Private Google Access provides access to Google APIs without external IP.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  VM["VM\n(No Ext IP)"] -->|"Cloud NAT"| Out["Internet"]
  style VM fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS has always been 'explicit by design' — private subnets never had default outbound. Azure historically gave all VMs implicit outbound via Microsoft-owned IPs, which was retired for new VNets (September 2025) , now requiring explicit egress configuration. GCP falls in between: the route exists but VMs without external IPs still need Cloud NAT.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's default outbound retirement required migration planning for many teams — VMs relying on implicit outbound lost internet access after reprovisioning without an explicit egress path. If you're migrating Azure workloads, audit every subnet for explicit egress paths. Engineers familiar with AWS's explicit-egress model noted that Azure's pre-2025 default outbound behaviour was a significant security posture difference.

azure.name: NAT Gateway
azure.description: Managed SNAT service providing outbound internet connectivity for VMs in a subnet. Standard SKU is zonal; StandardV2 (GA January 2026) is zone-redundant with up to 100 Gbps throughput and dual-stack (IPv4+IPv6) support. Applied at the subnet level with up to 16 public IPs for SNAT port scaling.
azure.keyDetail: $0.045/hr + $0.045/GB processed. Up to 16 public IPs (64,512 SNAT ports per IP). Zone-redundant. Subnet-level assignment.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  VM[VMs] --> NAT["NAT GW\nZone-redundant"] --> IP["Up to\n16 PIPs"]
  style NAT fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: NAT Gateway
aws.description: Managed NAT service providing outbound internet access for instances in private subnets. Supports up to 8 Elastic IPs per NAT Gateway for higher bandwidth and port capacity. Regional NAT Gateway (Nov 2025) eliminates the per-AZ deployment requirement for HA.
aws.keyDetail: $0.045/hr + $0.045/GB processed per gateway. 55,000 concurrent connections per gateway. Up to 8 EIPs per NAT Gateway. Up to 100 Gbps throughput. Regional NAT Gateway option available (Nov 2025) removes per-AZ requirement.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  VM[VMs] --> NAT["NAT GW\nMulti-EIP"] --> IP["Up to 8\nEIPs"]
  style NAT fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud NAT
gcp.description: Software-defined, distributed NAT service that operates at the regional level across all zones. No single gateway appliance — it is implemented in the network fabric itself, providing automatic scaling.
gcp.keyDetail: $0.0014/VM/hr, capped at $0.044/hr for 32+ VMs. Plus $0.045/GiB data processing charge.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  VM[VMs] --> NAT["Cloud NAT\nSoftware-defined"] --> IP["Auto\nIPs"]
  style NAT fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: GCP Cloud NAT is fundamentally different — it is software-defined in the network fabric with no dedicated gateway instance, providing automatic scaling and regional HA. AWS now offers both zonal and regional NAT Gateway options. The original zonal model requires one per AZ (multiplying cost for HA); the regional option (Nov 2025) simplifies this. Azure NAT Gateway is zone-redundant by default. Pricing is nearly identical across all three at ~$0.045/hr.
features.practitionerNote: AWS NAT Gateway (zonal model) requires one per AZ for HA, so a 3-AZ region costs 3x the base rate. The regional NAT Gateway option (Nov 2025) changes this calculus. Azure and GCP handle this natively. GCP's software-defined approach means there is no dedicated appliance — Cloud NAT is a configuration resource, not a provisioned instance, and scales automatically. NAT Gateway data processing charges are an often-overlooked cost on all three platforms.

azure.name: AVNM IPAM
azure.description: Native IP Address Management within Azure Virtual Network Manager. Provides centralized, hierarchical IP pool management with automatic non-overlapping CIDR allocation across subscriptions and regions.
azure.keyDetail: Hierarchical pools up to 7 levels deep. Direct integration with VNet/subnet creation. Visual audit and compliance tracking. GA November 2025.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  IPAM["AVNM\nIPAM"] --> Pool["IP Pool"]
  Pool --> VNet["VNet"]
  style IPAM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC IPAM
aws.description: Built-in enterprise-class IPAM for tracking, planning, and monitoring IP allocation across accounts and regions. Supports hierarchical structure with scopes, pools, and allocations.
aws.keyDetail: Hierarchical (IPAM > Scope > Pool > Allocation). Supports BYOIP integration. Multi-account via AWS Organizations. Longest-established of the three (released 2022).
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  IPAM["VPC\nIPAM"] --> Pool["Pool"]
  Pool --> VPC["VPC"]
  style IPAM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: No direct equivalent — closest is Internal Ranges and IP Address Management API
gcp.description: GCP does not offer a centralized, dedicated IPAM service comparable to Azure AVNM IPAM or AWS VPC IPAM. IP management is done through Internal Range objects, subnet reservations, and the Compute Engine API.
gcp.keyDetail: No native IPAM dashboard or hierarchical pool management. Relies on API/CLI for IP tracking. Third-party tools (NetBox, Infoblox) commonly used to fill the gap.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Manual["Manual\nPlanning"] --> VPC["VPC"]
  style Manual fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure and AWS both offer dedicated, centralized IPAM services with hierarchical pool management and compliance tracking. GCP has no equivalent native IPAM — you must use APIs, Internal Range objects, or third-party tools. AWS VPC IPAM is the longest-established (2022), Azure AVNM IPAM launched in 2024 and integrates with Azure Virtual Network Manager.
features.practitionerNote: IPAM becomes critical once environments grow beyond 200+ VNets, where overlapping CIDRs can break peering. AWS was first to solve this natively, Azure followed with AVNM IPAM, but GCP provides Internal Range objects and APIs for IP management but does not offer a dedicated IPAM console comparable to the AWS or Azure offerings. If you are multi-cloud, none of the native tools work cross-cloud — you need a third-party IPAM regardless.

azure.name: Custom IP Prefix (BYOIP)
azure.description: Bring your own public IP range to Azure. Three-phase process: validate, provision, commission. Supports hierarchical model with global parent prefix and regional child prefixes.
azure.keyDetail: IPv4: /21 to /24 (parent), /22 to /26 (child). IPv6: /48 (parent), /64 (child). Limit of 5 custom prefixes per region. No hourly IP billing for BYOIP ranges.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  BYOIP["Custom IP\nPrefix"] --> PIP["Public IPs"]
  style BYOIP fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: BYOIP (Bring Your Own IP)
aws.description: Import your own public IP address range into AWS for use with EC2, Global Accelerator, and other services. Requires ROA and RDAP certificate verification with your RIR.
aws.keyDetail: Minimum /24. No explicit maximum prefix limit. BYOIP addresses are exempt from AWS public IPv4 surcharge ($0.005/hr saving per IP). Provisioning takes 1-2 weeks.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  BYOIP["BYOIP"] --> EIP["Elastic IPs"]
  style BYOIP fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: BYOIP (Public Advertised Prefix)
gcp.description: Import your public IP range into GCP via Public Advertised Prefixes (PAP) and delegate to projects via Public Delegated Prefixes (PDP). Strict requirement that the range must not be announced elsewhere simultaneously.
gcp.keyDetail: Minimum /24. Provisioning takes 2-4 weeks (longest of the three). No IP charges for BYOIP ranges. Must stop external BGP announcements before GCP will provision.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  PAP["Public Advertised\nPrefix"] --> IP["External IPs"]
  style PAP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three require RIR registration and ROA creation with minimum /24 prefix. Azure supports a hierarchical parent/child model for splitting ranges across regions. GCP has the strictest exclusivity requirement — you must cease external announcements before provisioning. AWS is the fastest to provision (1-2 weeks) and exempts BYOIP from public IPv4 surcharges.
features.practitionerNote: BYOIP is mainly used for IP reputation preservation (email senders, API providers) and regulatory compliance. GCP's requirement to stop announcing elsewhere before provisioning makes blue-green migration harder than Azure or AWS. Azure's parent/child model is useful for splitting a /21 across multiple regions.

azure.name: Hub VNet + VNet Peering
azure.description: Traditional hub-spoke topology using a central hub VNet with shared services (firewall, gateways) peered to spoke VNets. Gateway transit allows spokes to share the hub's VPN/ExpressRoute gateway.
azure.keyDetail: Non-transitive by default — spoke-to-spoke requires UDR through hub NVA/firewall. Gateway transit reduces cost by sharing a single gateway. Up to 500 peerings per hub. Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) provides a modern managed alternative for hub-spoke topology management.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  H["Hub VNet\n+ Firewall"] --> S1[Spoke 1]
  H --> S2[Spoke 2]
  style H fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Transit Gateway (TGW)
aws.description: Managed regional hub that connects thousands of VPCs and on-premises networks. Provides transitive routing between all attachments via centralized route tables, replacing the need for individual VPC peerings.
aws.keyDetail: Supports 5,000+ attachments. Full transitive routing. Per-attachment route tables for segmentation. $0.05/hr per attachment + $0.02/GB processed.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  T["Transit\nGateway"] --> S1[VPC 1]
  T --> S2[VPC 2]
  style T fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Shared VPC / NCC Hub-and-Spoke
gcp.description: GCP offers two distinct patterns: Shared VPC shares a single VPC across projects (like a flat network, not a transit hub). Network Connectivity Center (NCC) provides hub-and-spoke transit with spokes connecting VPCs, VPN tunnels, and Cloud Interconnect attachments — this is the closer equivalent to Azure hub-spoke or AWS TGW.
gcp.keyDetail: Shared VPC: multi-project shared network — no transit routing, no firewall insertion point. NCC: true hub-and-spoke transit with BGP route exchange between spokes. NCC hub is global; spokes are regional. Key distinction: Shared VPC is an IAM/project model, NCC is a network topology model.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  H["Host\nProject"] --> S1[Service 1]
  H --> S2[Service 2]
  style H fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Fundamentally different approaches: Azure uses peering (non-transitive, requires NVA for spoke-to-spoke), AWS uses a managed transit hub (transitive, centralized routing), and GCP uses a shared network model (no peering at all — projects share the same VPC). GCP Shared VPC requires the least configuration but is the most tightly coupled; AWS TGW provides the most granular routing control via per-attachment route tables.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's peering-based hub-spoke is straightforward but increases in operational complexity at scale — spoke-to-spoke flows typically route through the hub firewall (architecture-dependent). AWS TGW is the most widely adopted managed transit service on AWS but costs add up with per-attachment and per-GB charges. GCP Shared VPC is architecturally simple but means all projects share the same network — blast radius is the whole VPC.

azure.name: Azure Virtual WAN
azure.description: Globally distributed managed networking hub that aggregates VNets, VPN, ExpressRoute, and SD-WAN connections across Azure's backbone. Automatic route propagation with built-in transit between all connected networks.
azure.keyDetail: Global multi-hub architecture. Automatic any-to-any routing. Integrated Azure Firewall. SD-WAN partner integration. Routing Intent (GA May 2023) simplifies inter-hub and internet routing via Azure Firewall or NVA next-hop. Less customisable than DIY hub-spoke but simpler to operate.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  H1["VWAN Hub\nWest EU"] <--> H2["VWAN Hub\nEast US"]
  H1 --> S[Spoke]
  H2 --> ER[ExpressRoute]
  style H1 fill:#0078D4,color:#fff
  style H2 fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Cloud WAN / Transit Gateway
aws.description: AWS Cloud WAN is the primary managed WAN service, providing a global network dashboard with automated policy-based routing across regions and accounts. Uses Transit Gateways as the underlying routing plane. TGW can also be used standalone for regional hub-and-spoke topologies.
aws.keyDetail: Cloud WAN: global policy-based network with automated routing. TGW: regional managed router, $0.05/hr per attachment. TGW peering for cross-region. Cloud WAN abstracts multi-region TGW mesh.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  CW["Cloud WAN\n(Global)"] --> T1["TGW\nus-east-1"]
  CW --> T2["TGW\neu-west-1"]
  T1 --> V[VPC]
  T2 --> DX[Direct Connect]
  style CW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff
  style T1 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff
  style T2 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Network Connectivity Center (NCC)
gcp.description: Global managed hub built on Google's premium backbone. Connects VPCs, VPNs, SD-WAN, and Interconnects via a single global hub with automatic routing leveraging Google's Andromeda SDN.
gcp.keyDetail: Global from day one (no regional peering needed). Leverages Google's premium backbone for lowest latency. Growing SD-WAN partner ecosystem.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NCC["NCC Hub"] --> S1["Spoke\nus-central1"]
  NCC --> S2["Spoke\neurope-west1"]
  style NCC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure VWAN and GCP NCC are both globally managed with automatic routing. AWS TGW is regional and requires explicit inter-region peering for global connectivity. AWS offers the most granular routing control; Azure VWAN integrates routing, firewall, and VPN management in a single resource; GCP NCC leverages the extensive backbone but is the newest of the three, with a growing third-party integration ecosystem.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Virtual WAN is the most prescriptive — it manages routing for you, which simplifies standard topologies but reduces flexibility for non-standard patterns (asymmetric routing, custom next-hops). AWS TGW provides the most granular control, which requires careful route table design. GCP NCC is the newest and expanding its third-party integrations.

azure.name: User-Defined Routes (UDR)
azure.description: Custom routes applied via route tables associated with subnets. Override system routes to direct traffic through NVAs, firewalls, or other next hops. Can disable BGP route propagation per route table.
azure.keyDetail: 400 routes per route table (up to 1,000 with AVNM UDR management). Applied at subnet level. Can override system routes and disable BGP propagation.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  UDR["UDR\n(400 limit)"] --> Sub["Subnet"]
  style UDR fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Route Tables
aws.description: Each subnet is associated with exactly one route table containing static routes. Routes define next hops for destination CIDRs. Default limit of 500 routes per table (1,000 max) since June 2025.
aws.keyDetail: 500 routes per route table by default (up to 1,000 max). Each subnet gets exactly one route table. No BGP propagation toggle — routes are static or gateway-propagated. Limits increased June 2025.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  RT["Route Table\n(500 limit)"] --> Sub["Subnet"]
  style RT fill:#FF9900,color:#fff
aws.practitionerNote: Route table limits were significantly increased in June 2025 from 50/100 to 500/1,000. This addresses a previous limitation when comparing AWS to Azure/GCP route capacity.

gcp.name: Routes
gcp.description: Routes are global resources within a VPC, not per-subnet. Static routes and policy-based routes can direct traffic to specific next hops. Dynamic routes from Cloud Router are automatically distributed.
gcp.keyDetail: 250 static routes per VPC. 300 dynamic (Cloud Router learned) routes per region per VPC. Routes are global within the VPC — no per-subnet route tables.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Route["Route\n(Global)"] --> VPC["VPC Network"]
  style Route fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure applies routes per subnet via route tables (400-1000 routes). AWS also uses per-subnet route tables but with a 500 routes per table (raised from 50 in June 2025). GCP routes are VPC-wide and global — there are no per-subnet route tables. This makes GCP routing simpler but less granular. Azure supports 400 routes per table by default (increasable to 1,000); AWS supports 500 per table.
features.practitionerNote: AWS raised its default route-table limit from 50 to 500 in June 2025, easing constraints for SD-WAN scenarios — though you may still hit it in very complex environments. Azure's 400 is generous but still matters with large on-premises route tables. GCP's global route model means you cannot have different routing behavior per subnet without using policy-based routes, which are a newer addition to the platform.

azure.name: Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM)
azure.description: Centralized management service for network connectivity, security policies, and routing across VNets at scale. Uses group-based targeting to apply configurations to thousands of VNets across subscriptions and regions.
azure.keyDetail: Group-based policy targeting. Security admin rules (higher priority than NSGs). Connected group mesh and hub-spoke topologies. IPAM and UDR management built in.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  AVNM["AVNM"] --> Rules["Security\nAdmin Rules"]
  AVNM --> Groups["Network\nGroups"]
  style AVNM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Network Manager + Cloud WAN
aws.description: Network Manager provides global topology visualization and monitoring. AWS Cloud WAN extends this with a managed global core network and segment-based routing policies, succeeding the Transit Gateway for new global deployments.
aws.keyDetail: Global topology view across accounts. Cloud WAN provides policy-based global routing with segments. Integrates with Transit Gateway, VPN, and Direct Connect.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  CW["Cloud WAN"] --> NM["Network\nManager"]
  NM --> Seg["Segments"]
  style CW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Network Connectivity Center (NCC)
gcp.description: Serves double duty as both the managed WAN hub and the centralized network management plane. Combines connectivity management with topology visualization across VPCs, hybrid connections, and SD-WAN.
gcp.keyDetail: Single service for both connectivity and management. Global hub-and-spoke with automatic routing. Less separation between management plane and data plane than Azure/AWS.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NCC["NCC"] --> Hub["Hub"]
  Hub --> Spoke["Spokes"]
  style NCC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure AVNM focuses on policy-at-scale, offering security admin rules, connected groups, and centralized IPAM. AWS separates monitoring (Network Manager) from connectivity (Cloud WAN/TGW), giving more flexibility. GCP combines management and connectivity in NCC, which has a different feature scope — governance and compliance controls are managed separately through GCP Organization Policies.
features.practitionerNote: AVNM's security admin rules sit above NSGs in priority, so a central team can enforce deny rules that workload teams cannot override. AWS handles centralized security through SCP and Firewall Manager; GCP uses Organization Policies and Hierarchical Firewall Rules. AWS Cloud WAN is the successor to Transit Gateway for new deployments but many orgs still run TGW.

azure.name: AVNM Connected Groups
azure.description: Create a mesh of directly connected VNets without individual peering connections. VNets in a connected group get full bidirectional connectivity managed centrally by AVNM, with optional hub-spoke or mesh topology.
azure.keyDetail: Full mesh without managing individual peerings. Up to 250 VNets per connected group (soft limit, raisable to 1,000 via support request). Spoke-to-spoke direct connectivity. Policy-driven membership.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  CG["Connected\nGroup"] --> V1["VNet 1"]
  CG --> V2["VNet 2"]
  CG --> V3["VNet 3"]
  style CG fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: No direct equivalent — closest is Transit Gateway with route table associations
aws.description: AWS achieves similar outcomes via Transit Gateway route tables and associations. VPCs in the same route domain can communicate transitively. AWS Cloud WAN segments provide policy-based grouping for newer deployments.
aws.keyDetail: TGW route tables provide segmentation/grouping. Cloud WAN segments offer policy-based grouping. Requires explicit route table configuration, unlike Azure Connected Groups' policy-driven model.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  TGW["TGW"] --> RT1["Route\nTable 1"]
  TGW --> RT2["Route\nTable 2"]
  style TGW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: No direct equivalent — closest is NCC spoke groups or Shared VPC service projects
gcp.description: GCP achieves transit connectivity via Shared VPC (all projects in same network) or NCC hub-and-spoke. No concept of dynamically grouping separate VPCs into a mesh without peering or a shared network.
gcp.keyDetail: Shared VPC (IAM/project model, not mesh connectivity) provides implicit mesh (same network). NCC connects VPC spokes through a hub with spoke groups and auto-accept for VPC-to-VPC connectivity. No concept of dynamically grouping separate VPCs into a mesh without peering or a hub.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Host["Host\nProject"] --> P1["Service\nProject 1"]
  Host --> P2["Service\nProject 2"]
  style Host fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure AVNM Connected Groups creates direct mesh connectivity between VNets without individual peering, managed by policy — it creates direct mesh connectivity between VNets without individual peering, managed by policy. AWS requires Transit Gateway route table configuration to achieve similar grouping. GCP relies on Shared VPC (same network) or NCC. Each approach has different operational trade-offs at scale.
features.practitionerNote: Without Connected Groups (or a similar abstraction), meshing 50 VNets in a mesh would require 1,225 individual peerings, but Connected Groups does it with one configuration. The 250 VNet limit per group is sufficient for most enterprise topologies. AWS achieves similar outcomes through TGW route table configuration, which provides more granular control but requires more explicit setup.

azure.name: Azure Route Server
azure.description: Managed BGP route reflector that enables dynamic route exchange between NVAs (firewalls, SD-WAN) and Azure VNet system routes. Eliminates the need for static UDRs when using NVAs, and supports transitive routing between peers. This feature focuses on the dynamic routing capability that Route Server provides to VNets.
azure.keyDetail: Requires dedicated /27 subnet. Supports ECMP. Up to 8 BGP peers. Enables NVA-advertised routes to propagate to VNet and peered VNets. Per-VNet deployment.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  NVA[NVA] <-->|BGP| RS[Route Server]
  RS -->|Injects| RT[VNet Routes]
  style RS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Route Server
aws.description: Managed BGP router within a VPC (GA April 2025), enabling dynamic route exchange with NVAs. Direct equivalent of Azure Route Server. $0.75/hr. Propagates learned routes to VPC route tables.
aws.keyDetail: GA since April 2025 — direct equivalent to Azure Route Server. $0.75/hr per Route Server. BGP peering with NVAs inside a VPC. Propagates routes to VPC route tables dynamically.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  NVA[Appliance] <-->|BGP| VRS[VPC Route Server]
  VRS -->|Dynamic| RT[Route Table]
  style VRS fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud Router
gcp.description: Fully managed BGP router tightly integrated with VPC, Cloud VPN, Interconnect, and NCC. Learns and advertises routes dynamically. Regional deployment with global or regional dynamic routing mode.
gcp.keyDetail: Fully managed — no dedicated subnet needed. Supports BFD, MD5 auth, and route filtering policies. Integrated with Interconnect and VPN. Longest-established of the three.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NVA[NVA] <-->|BGP| CR[Cloud Router]
  CR -->|Programmatic| RT[VPC Routes]
  style CR fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: GCP Cloud Router is the longest-established and tightly integrated with VPN, Interconnect, and NCC — it handles BGP for VPN, Interconnect, and NVAs in one service. Azure Route Server focuses specifically on NVA-to-VNet BGP integration and is well-established. AWS VPC Route Server is the newest (2025) and provides VPC-native BGP integration, whereas previously Transit Gateway Connect was the standard approach.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Route Server enables dynamic route exchange between SD-WAN appliances and VNet route tables, replacing static UDR management — without it, you must maintain static UDRs for each route. GCP Cloud Router has done this since its early days and is the mature BGP implementation of the three. AWS VPC Route Server shipped in 2025, providing direct VPC-level BGP peering. Previously, Transit Gateway Connect was the standard approach for NVA BGP integration.

azure.name: Azure VPN Gateway (Site-to-Site)
azure.description: Managed IPsec/IKE VPN gateway supporting up to 100 S2S tunnels per gateway with SKUs from VpnGw1 to VpnGw5. Zone-redundant AZ SKUs available.
azure.keyDetail: Up to 10 Gbps aggregate throughput (VpnGw5AZ). Supports active-active mode, BGP, and static routing. IKEv1/IKEv2. Non-AZ VPN Gateway SKUs deprecated (announced Nov 2025, migration deadline Sep 30, 2026) — all new deployments should use AZ SKUs (VpnGw1AZ, etc.).
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  OnP[On-prem] <-->|IPsec/IKEv2| VGW["VPN\nGateway"]
  style VGW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Site-to-Site VPN
aws.description: Managed IPsec VPN with two tunnels per connection for built-in HA. Attaches to Virtual Private Gateway (single VPC) or Transit Gateway (multi-VPC).
aws.keyDetail: 1.25 Gbps per standard tunnel, 5 Gbps with Large Bandwidth Tunnels (TGW and Cloud WAN). Active-active by default. 50 connections per region (soft limit).
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  OnP[On-prem] <-->|IPsec| VGW["Site-to-Site\nVPN"]
  style VGW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP HA VPN (Cloud VPN)
gcp.description: High-availability VPN with two interfaces per gateway and 99.99% SLA. Classic VPN is deprecated in favour of HA VPN.
gcp.keyDetail: Up to 3 Gbps per tunnel. Standard 2-tunnel HA gateway gives ~6 Gbps via ECMP. Scale horizontally. BGP-only for HA VPN (no static routes). IKEv1 and IKEv2 (IKEv2 required for IPv6, recommended).
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  OnP[On-prem] <-->|IPsec + BGP| VPN["HA VPN\n99.99% SLA"]
  style VPN fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure offers the broadest SKU range and the highest single-gateway throughput (10 Gbps). AWS provides automatic dual-tunnel HA but lower per-tunnel bandwidth. GCP mandates BGP for HA VPN, making it the most prescriptive HA model, with a 99.99% SLA out of the box with a 99.99% SLA out of the box.
features.practitionerNote: When connecting clouds together (e.g. Azure-to-AWS VPN), ensure IKE versions and DH groups match on both sides. GCP's BGP-only requirement for HA VPN means you can't use static routes — plan your ASN allocation early.

azure.name: Azure Point-to-Site VPN
azure.description: Remote access VPN for individual clients connecting to Azure VNets. Supports OpenVPN, SSTP, and IKEv2 protocols.
azure.keyDetail: Authenticates via Entra ID (Azure AD), RADIUS, or certificates. Up to 10,000 P2S connections on VpnGw5. Windows/macOS/Linux clients.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  User["User"] --> GW["P2S VPN\nGateway"]
  GW --> VNet["VNet"]
  style GW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Client VPN
aws.description: Fully managed OpenVPN-based remote access VPN for connecting users to AWS VPCs and on-premises networks.
aws.keyDetail: OpenVPN protocol only. Supports Active Directory, SAML 2.0, and mutual certificate auth. Elastically scalable. Split tunnelling supported.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  User["User"] --> CV["Client\nVPN"]
  CV --> VPC["VPC"]
  style CV fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: No direct P2S equivalent — closest: Chrome Enterprise Premium / IAP TCP Tunnelling
gcp.description: GCP has no native network-level P2S VPN service. IAP TCP Tunnelling provides per-application zero-trust access (not full network connectivity). Chrome Enterprise Premium (formerly BeyondCorp Enterprise) provides clientless zero-trust access to web apps. For full network-level P2S, third-party VPN appliances are needed.
gcp.keyDetail: IAP TCP Tunnelling: single-port TCP forwarding through Google's edge, requires IAM auth per connection. Not a network tunnel — no IP assignment, no split tunnelling, no routing. Chrome Enterprise Premium: browser-based zero-trust access. Neither provides the full L3 VPN experience of Azure P2S or AWS Client VPN.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  User["User"] --> IAP["IAP TCP\nTunnel"]
  IAP --> VM["VM"]
  style IAP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure and AWS offer traditional network-level VPN clients giving full subnet access. GCP takes a fundamentally different zero-trust approach with IAP — no VPN client, application-level granularity, but not suitable for legacy TCP/UDP protocols that need raw network access. GCP has no direct P2S VPN equivalent.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's IAP approach is architecturally distinct — using identity-based access rather than network tunnels — well-suited for modern web workloads, but requires additional configuration for RDP, SSH to arbitrary IPs, or database access. For GCP, consider using IAP tunnels for SSH/RDP and Tailscale/WireGuard for broader network access.

azure.name: Azure ExpressRoute (via Provider)
azure.description: Private Layer 3 connection to Azure via a connectivity provider (telco or exchange). Supports private peering (VNets) and Microsoft peering (M365/Dynamics).
azure.keyDetail: 50 Mbps to 10 Gbps via providers. Dual redundant circuits for SLA. Metered or unlimited data plans. 12 months to provision in some regions.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  DC[On-prem] --> P[Provider] --> ER[ExpressRoute]
  style ER fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Direct Connect (Hosted Connection)
aws.description: Private dedicated connection to AWS via an approved partner. Hosted connections offer sub-port bandwidth allocations from partner facilities.
aws.keyDetail: 50 Mbps to 10 Gbps hosted connections. Private VIF (VPC), Public VIF (AWS public services), Transit VIF (Transit Gateway). Per-GB egress pricing.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  DC[On-prem] --> P[Partner] --> DX[Direct Connect]
  style DX fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Partner Interconnect
gcp.description: Private connectivity to GCP via an approved service provider. Suitable for locations where Dedicated Interconnect is not available.
gcp.keyDetail: 50 Mbps to 50 Gbps per VLAN attachment. Multiple attachments for redundancy. Discounted egress pricing vs internet. Layer 2 or Layer 3 partner models.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  DC[On-prem] --> P[Partner] --> PI[Partner Interconnect]
  style PI fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure offers Microsoft Peering for O365/SaaS traffic alongside private peering on the same circuit. AWS uses distinct VIF types (Private/Public/Transit) for different traffic classes. GCP Partner Interconnect supports the widest per-attachment bandwidth (up to 50 Gbps) and benefits from global VPC routing. All three require redundant circuits for SLA.
features.practitionerNote: Using ExpressRoute for M365 now requires explicit Microsoft approval and is discouraged — Microsoft now recommends internet-based connectivity for M365. Factor this into long-term circuit planning.

azure.name: Azure ExpressRoute Direct
azure.description: Customer-owned dual 10G or 100G ports directly to Microsoft edge routers at peering locations. Enables multiple ExpressRoute circuits on a single port pair.
azure.keyDetail: Dual 10 Gbps or dual 100 Gbps ports. Supports MACsec encryption. Multiple circuits per port pair. ~$40-50k/month for dual 100G (US). 400G port pairs have been announced.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  DC["Colo"] --> ERD["ER Direct\n(Port Pair)"]
  ERD --> MSEE["Microsoft"]
  style ERD fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Direct Connect (Dedicated Connection)
aws.description: Physical cross-connect from customer/colo router directly to AWS equipment at a Direct Connect location.
aws.keyDetail: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps, or 400 Gbps dedicated ports. Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs) for higher throughput. MACsec supported on select locations.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  DC["Colo"] --> DX["Dedicated\nConnection"]
  DX --> AWS["AWS"]
  DX -.- Speeds["1/10/100/400G"]
  style DX fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Dedicated Interconnect
gcp.description: Physical connection from customer router to Google edge at a colocation facility. Highest bandwidth and lowest latency option. Now available at 10G, 100G, and 400G.
gcp.keyDetail: 10G, 100G, or 400G per link. Up to 8×400G (3.2 Tbps) per location. MACsec supported. Contact Google sales for port pricing.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  DC["Colo"] --> DI["Dedicated\nInterconnect"]
  DI --> GCP["Google"]
  style DI fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure ExpressRoute Direct is the only service that allowing multiple logical circuits over a single physical port pair, useful for segregating production/dev or different business units. AWS offers 1G dedicated ports (Azure/GCP minimum is 10G). GCP supports the highest aggregate bandwidth per location at 800 Gbps via LAG. All three support MACsec.
features.practitionerNote: ExpressRoute Direct's multi-circuit-per-port model is well-suited for managed service providers or enterprises needing traffic isolation without separate physical cross-connects. AWS's 1G dedicated option provides a lower bandwidth tier that Azure and GCP do not offer.

azure.name: Azure ExpressRoute Direct MACsec
azure.description: IEEE 802.1AE Layer 2 encryption on ExpressRoute Direct ports between customer and Microsoft edge routers.
azure.keyDetail: Supports GCM-AES-128, GCM-AES-256, GCM-AES-XPN-128, GCM-AES-XPN-256
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  ERD["ER Direct"] -->|"MACsec"| MSEE["MSEE"]
  style ERD fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Direct Connect MACsec
aws.description: MACsec encryption on Direct Connect connections at supported locations. Available on Dedicated Connections and partner-delivered connections. Supports 1/10/100/400 Gbps ports. Ciphers include GCM-AES-256 and GCM-AES-XPN-256.
aws.keyDetail: GCM-AES-256 and GCM-AES-XPN-256 ciphers. Static CAK mode only. Available on 10/100/400 Gbps Dedicated Connections and partner connections. Not all locations support MACsec.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  DX["DX Port"] -->|"MACsec"| AWS["AWS Router"]
  style DX fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Cloud Interconnect MACsec
gcp.description: MACsec encryption on both Dedicated and Partner Interconnect at 10, 100, and 400 Gbps speeds.
gcp.keyDetail: GCM-AES-256 cipher. No additional cost for MACsec. Supported on Dedicated and Partner Interconnect at 10G, 100G, and 400G. 10G MACsec requires account team engagement. Keys provisioned via Cloud Console.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  IC["Interconnect"] -->|"MACsec"| GCP["Google Router"]
  style IC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: GCP leads here with MACsec on both Dedicated and Partner Interconnect at no extra cost, plus 400 Gbps support and AES-256. AWS also uses AES-256 but only on Dedicated connections at select locations. Azure supports GCM-AES-128 and GCM-AES-256 on Direct ports only. All three support AES-256. None encrypt traffic within their backbone — MACsec is last-mile only.
features.practitionerNote: Verify Azure's current MACsec cipher support in documentation, as capabilities have expanded. For MACsec on partner circuits (not just direct), GCP remains the only option. If you need wire-speed encryption on provider circuits (not just direct), GCP is the only option. For end-to-end encryption across the cloud backbone, layer IPsec/TLS on top regardless of provider.

azure.name: Azure ExpressRoute Global Reach
azure.description: Enables on-premises sites connected via separate ExpressRoute circuits in different regions to communicate directly over the Microsoft backbone, bypassing the public internet.
azure.keyDetail: Site-to-site traffic hairpins through Microsoft edge — no Azure VNet needed in the path. Available in most public regions. ExpressRoute Premium add-on required only when connecting circuits across different geopolitical regions; same-region Global Reach works with Standard SKU.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  Site1["Site 1"] --> GR["Global\nReach"] --> Site2["Site 2"]
  style GR fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Direct Connect Gateway
aws.description: Allows a single Direct Connect circuit to reach VPCs in multiple AWS regions. Not a direct equivalent to Global Reach — it routes to AWS VPCs, not between on-prem sites.
aws.keyDetail: Connect VPCs across regions via one DX circuit. Does NOT enable site-to-site transit between on-prem locations over AWS backbone. Requires Transit Gateway for inter-region traffic.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  DXGW["DX\nGateway"] --> R1["Region 1"]
  DXGW --> R2["Region 2"]
  style DXGW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Global VPC + Cloud Interconnect (Global Routing)
gcp.description: GCP's global VPC model means a single Interconnect attachment can reach resources in any region. No separate 'Global Reach' product needed — it's inherent in VPC design.
gcp.keyDetail: Enable 'Global Routing' on VPC to advertise all subnets across regions. Single Interconnect serves all regions. No equivalent of Azure's site-to-site hairpin feature.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  VPC["Global VPC"] --> R1["Region 1"]
  VPC --> R2["Region 2"]
  style VPC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Global Reach's the only service that directly connects two on-premises locations via the cloud backbone without transiting a VNet. AWS Direct Connect Gateway routes to VPCs, not between on-prem sites. GCP's global VPC provides cross-region reach natively but also doesn't offer site-to-site transit.
features.practitionerNote: Global Reach addresses a specific use case for enterprises replacing MPLS between branch offices. AWS and GCP require NVA or SD-WAN overlays to replicate this — you'd need an NVA or SD-WAN overlay to achieve similar site-to-site transit on those platforms.

azure.name: Azure Route Server
azure.description: Managed BGP route exchange service enabling NVAs (firewalls, SD-WAN routers) to dynamically exchange routes with Azure VNet system routes and gateways. This feature focuses on Route Server as a route exchange platform between NVAs and Azure networking (ExpressRoute, VPN gateways).
azure.keyDetail: BGP peering with up to 8 NVAs. Propagates routes between NVAs, ExpressRoute, and VPN gateways. Enables transitive routing. Requires /27 subnet. $0.45/hr.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  RS["Route\nServer"] -->|"BGP"| NVA["NVA"]
  RS --> VNet["VNet Routes"]
  style RS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Transit Gateway (+ Connect)
aws.description: No direct equivalent to Route Server. Transit Gateway serves as a central routing hub. TGW Connect provides GRE+BGP attachments for SD-WAN/NVA integration.
aws.keyDetail: TGW Connect enables BGP peering with in-VPC appliances via GRE tunnels. More complex than Azure Route Server but more feature-rich with per-attachment route tables.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  TGW["TGW\nConnect"] -->|"GRE+BGP"| App["Appliance"]
  style TGW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Cloud Router
gcp.description: Managed BGP service that dynamically exchanges routes between VPC networks and on-premises/partner networks via VPN or Interconnect.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports 16-bit and 32-bit ASNs. Dynamic route advertisement — subnets auto-propagated. Regional by default but serves global VPC. Advanced route policies and BFD support.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  CR["Cloud\nRouter"] -->|"BGP"| NVA["NVA"]
  style CR fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Route Server is purpose-built for in-cloud NVA BGP peering — a unique service with no exact AWS/GCP parallel. AWS requires Transit Gateway Connect (GRE+BGP) for similar NVA integration, which is more powerful but complex. GCP Cloud Router is edge-focused (VPN/Interconnect to VPC) and less suited for in-cloud NVA peering.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Route Server is enables dynamic injection of third-party routes into Azure VNet route tables via BGP. On AWS, TGW Connect requires additional GRE tunnel configuration alongside BGP. On GCP, the global VPC model reduces the need for in-cloud route exchange, but NVA scenarios require manual UDR management.

azure.name: Azure IPsec over ExpressRoute
azure.description: Run an IPsec VPN tunnel over ExpressRoute for double encryption. Supported over both private peering (most common modern pattern) and Microsoft peering. The VPN tunnel uses ExpressRoute as the transport instead of the public internet.
azure.keyDetail: Supported on both ExpressRoute private peering and Microsoft peering. VPN Gateway and ExpressRoute Gateway coexist in the same VNet. Throughput limited by VPN Gateway SKU, not the circuit.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  Site["On-prem"] -->|"IPsec\nover ER"| GW["VPN GW"]
  style GW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Site-to-Site VPN over Direct Connect
aws.description: Run an IPsec VPN tunnel over a Direct Connect connection for double encryption. The modern approach uses Private IP VPN over a Transit VIF with Transit Gateway, keeping all traffic on private addressing.
aws.keyDetail: Modern pattern: Private IP VPN over Transit VIF (recommended). Legacy pattern used Public VIF — no longer required. Throughput limited by VPN tunnel limits (up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel). Requires Transit Gateway with Direct Connect Gateway.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  Site["On-prem"] -->|"VPN over\nDX Transit VIF"| TGW["Transit GW"]
  style TGW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP HA VPN over Cloud Interconnect
gcp.description: Deploy HA VPN gateways that use Interconnect VLAN attachments as the transport layer instead of public internet, combining private transport with IPsec encryption.
gcp.keyDetail: Supported on both Dedicated and Partner Interconnect. Provides 99.99% SLA when properly configured. BGP required. Throughput scales with number of tunnels.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  Site["On-prem"] -->|"HA VPN over\nInterconnect"| GW["VPN GW"]
  style GW fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three support VPN-over-private-circuit for compliance-driven double encryption. Azure uses private peering as transport (simplest model). AWS requires a Transit VIF (Private IP VPN) for the VPN underlay, which adds complexity. GCP's HA VPN over Interconnect is the most modern implementation with native HA and no SLA compromise.
features.practitionerNote: This pattern is mainly for regulatory compliance (e.g. government, finance) where private transport alone isn't sufficient. The VPN throughput becomes the bottleneck — not the circuit. On AWS, the VPN-over-Direct Connect architecture has evolved — verify whether your design uses the older Public VIF approach or the newer Private IP VPN via Transit VIF that is a common point of confusion.

azure.name: Azure ExpressRoute Gateway SKUs
azure.description: ErGw1AZ, ErGw2AZ, ErGw3AZ tiers plus the newer ErGwScale (GA) for ExpressRoute. VPN Gateway uses separate VpnGw1-5 SKUs.
azure.keyDetail: ErGwScale (GA): autoscaling ExpressRoute gateway with FastPath support, zone redundancy, and up to 40 Gbps throughput. ErGw1AZ/2AZ/3AZ: fixed-tier legacy SKUs (1/2/10 Gbps). VPN Gateway SKUs: VpnGw1-5(AZ), 650 Mbps to 10 Gbps aggregate VPN throughput.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  SKU["ErGw SKUs"] --> S1["Standard"]
  SKU --> S2["ErGwScale"]
  style SKU fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Direct Connect Port Speeds
aws.description: No gateway SKU concept — bandwidth is determined by the physical port (1G, 10G, 100G, 400G) and VIF allocation. VPN throughput is per-tunnel.
aws.keyDetail: Dedicated ports: 1/10/100/400 Gbps. Hosted: 50 Mbps–10 Gbps. VPN tunnels: 1.25 Gbps standard, 5 Gbps large bandwidth. No gateway VM bottleneck for DX.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  Port["DX Port"] --> S1["1 Gbps"]
  Port --> S2["10/100 Gbps"]
  Port --> S3["400 Gbps"]
  style Port fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: GCP Interconnect Port Speeds
gcp.description: No gateway SKU concept — Interconnect bandwidth scales with physical ports and VLAN attachments. Cloud Router handles routing at no additional bandwidth constraint.
gcp.keyDetail: Dedicated: 10G, 100G, or 400G per link, up to 8×400G (3.2 Tbps). Partner: 50 Mbps–50 Gbps. VPN: 3 Gbps per tunnel, 12 Gbps per HA gateway.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  IC["Interconnect"] --> S1["10 Gbps"]
  IC --> S2["100 Gbps"]
  style IC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure is the only service that routes traffic through a gateway VM that caps throughput — all ErGw*AZ SKUs cap at 10 Gbps regardless of circuit size. AWS and GCP have no equivalent gateway bottleneck; throughput scales with the physical port. This means a 100G ExpressRoute Direct circuit still flows through a 10 Gbps gateway (FastPath bypasses this for some traffic). AWS and GCP do not have an equivalent gateway-based throughput constraint.
features.practitionerNote: The 10 Gbps ErGw gateway cap is a throughput constraint to account for to account for when sizing Azure deployments. FastPath helps by bypassing the gateway for data-plane traffic, but control-plane operations still transit it. ErGwScale is now GA (November 2025) and addresses this with up to 40 Gbps and autoscaling. When sizing, remember: circuit bandwidth ≠ gateway throughput in Azure.

azure.name: Azure Firewall
azure.description: Managed, cloud-native network firewall with built-in HA, unrestricted scalability, and FQDN filtering. Available in Standard, Premium (IDPS, TLS inspection), and Basic SKUs.
azure.keyDetail: Basic: $0.395/hr, Standard: $1.25/hr (~$0.016/GB data processing), Premium: $1.75/hr (~$0.016/GB data processing). All SKUs incur additional per-GB charges for data processed.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  FW["Azure Firewall\nL3-L7 + IDPS"] --> P["Premium:\nTLS Inspect"]
  style FW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Network Firewall
aws.description: Managed stateful and stateless network firewall service for VPCs. Uses Suricata-compatible rule engine for L3-L7 inspection including IDS/IPS.
aws.keyDetail: Deploys per-AZ endpoints within VPCs. Suricata rule engine allows custom IDS/IPS signatures. ~$0.395/hr per endpoint + $0.065/GB processed. Integrates with AWS Firewall Manager.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  FW["Network Firewall\nSuricata Engine"] --> T["TLS +\nCustom Rules"]
  style FW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud Next Generation Firewall (Cloud NGFW)
gcp.description: Distributed, cloud-native firewall with three tiers: Essentials (free L3/L4 rules), Standard (FQDN filtering, geo-location filtering, threat intelligence), and Enterprise (L7 IDPS/URL filtering powered by Palo Alto Networks).
gcp.keyDetail: $0.0193/GiB + $1.75/hr per firewall endpoint (Enterprise)
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  FW["Cloud NGFW\nPalo Alto"] --> E["Enterprise:\nL7 + TLS"]
  style FW fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Firewall is a dedicated VNet resource with a private IP; AWS Network Firewall uses per-AZ endpoints within VPCs; GCP Cloud NGFW is distributed across the VPC fabric with no discrete resource to deploy. GCP's Enterprise tier uniquely leverages Palo Alto Networks technology natively. All three scored poorly in CyberRatings.org 2025 independent intrusion testing.
features.practitionerNote: All three native firewalls provide adequate capability for network segmentation and compliance, but for advanced threat prevention (IDS/IPS), many enterprises still deploy third-party NVAs (Palo Alto, Fortinet). Evaluate whether the native IDPS signatures actually cover your threat model.

azure.name: Azure DDoS Protection
azure.description: Three tiers: Infrastructure Protection (free, automatic), IP Protection ($199/IP/month), and Network Protection ($2,944/month for 100 IPs with DDoS Response Team and cost protection).
azure.keyDetail: DDoS Network Protection: ~$2,944/month covers up to 100 public IPs. Includes DDoS Rapid Response team, attack analytics, and cost guarantee for scaling during attacks.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  DDoS["DDoS\nProtection"] --> VNet["VNet"]
  VNet --> PIP["Public IPs"]
  style DDoS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Shield
aws.description: Two tiers: Shield Standard (free, automatic L3/L4 protection for all AWS resources) and Shield Advanced ($3,000/month per organization with L7 protection, DRT access, cost protection).
aws.keyDetail: Shield Advanced at $3,000/month per organization (not per account) includes 24/7 DDoS Response Team, real-time metrics, WAF integration, and financial cost protection for DDoS-induced scaling. Covers CloudFront, Route 53, ALB, ELB.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  Shield["Shield\nAdvanced"] --> Res["ELB / CF /\nRoute 53"]
  style Shield fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Google Cloud Armor
gcp.description: Always-on infrastructure DDoS protection for resources behind external load balancers. Cloud Armor Enterprise adds advanced DDoS detection, adaptive protection with ML, and managed WAF rules.
gcp.keyDetail: Standard DDoS protection is free for external LBs. Cloud Armor Enterprise adds ML-based adaptive protection, named IP lists, and threat intelligence. Leverages Google's massive backbone for absorption.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Armor["Cloud\nArmor"] --> LB["Load\nBalancer"]
  style Armor fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS Shield Advanced and Azure DDoS Protection both offer dedicated response teams and financial cost guarantees — GCP Cloud Armor does not. GCP's DDoS protection is tightly coupled with Cloud Armor WAF (same product), while Azure and AWS separate DDoS and WAF into distinct services. All three absorb attacks at the network edge using global backbone capacity.
features.practitionerNote: The cost protection guarantee in AWS Shield Advanced and Azure DDoS Protection is a key differentiator — it reimburses you for autoscaling costs during an attack. GCP Cloud Armor does not include a comparable cost protection guarantee. For most workloads, the free tiers on all three clouds handle common volumetric attacks fine.

azure.name: Azure WAF
azure.description: Available on Azure Application Gateway (regional) and Azure Front Door (global). Supports OWASP CRS managed rulesets, custom rules, bot protection, and rate limiting.
azure.keyDetail: AppGW WAFv2: $0.443/hr + capacity units. Front Door: bundled in tier price. Classic: $5/policy + $1/rule + $0.60/M requests.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  WAF["WAF Policy"] --> FD["Front Door"]
  WAF --> AGW["App GW"]
  style WAF fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS WAF
aws.description: Sits in front of CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, and AppSync. Uses Web ACLs with managed rule groups (AWS + marketplace), custom rules, and advanced bot control.
aws.keyDetail: Pay per Web ACL ($5/month), per rule ($1/month), and per million requests ($0.60). Advanced Bot Control available. Marketplace rule groups from F5, Fortinet, etc. Deeply integrated with Shield Advanced.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  WAF["AWS WAF"] --> ALB["ALB"]
  WAF --> CF["CloudFront"]
  style WAF fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Google Cloud Armor (WAF)
gcp.description: Deployed at the edge in front of HTTP(S) Load Balancers. Provides preconfigured OWASP CRS rules, custom rules, rate limiting, and adaptive protection with ML-based anomaly detection.
gcp.keyDetail: WAF and DDoS are combined in a single Cloud Armor product. Preconfigured rules for OWASP Top 10. Enterprise tier adds adaptive protection using ML to auto-detect L7 attacks. Per-policy + per-rule pricing.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  Armor["Cloud Armor\nWAF"] --> LB["HTTPS LB"]
  style Armor fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure deploys WAF on two separate products (Application Gateway for regional, Front Door for global edge). AWS WAF has the richest marketplace of third-party managed rule groups. GCP Cloud Armor uniquely combines WAF + DDoS in one product with ML-powered adaptive protection. All three support OWASP CRS-based rulesets.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's adaptive protection (ML-based) is differentiated — it detects anomalous L7 traffic patterns automatically. AWS has the largest third-party rule ecosystem via its marketplace. Azure's dual deployment model (AppGW vs Front Door) requires careful selection — generally prefer Front Door WAF for global apps.

azure.name: Azure Bastion
azure.description: Managed jump host providing browser-based RDP/SSH to Azure VMs over TLS. VMs require no public IP, no agent, and no open inbound ports. Available in Developer (free, limited), Basic, Standard, and Premium SKUs.
azure.keyDetail: Developer SKU: free, limited features. Standard: ~$0.29/hr, adds native client/CLI and IP-based connections. Premium: adds session recording, private-only access, and Shareable Links. Requires deployment per VNet.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  B["Azure Bastion\nManaged PaaS"] --> VM["VM\nNo Public IP"]
  style B fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager
aws.description: Secure shell/RDP access via the SSM Agent installed on EC2 instances (preinstalled on Amazon Linux 2/2023 and Windows Server AMIs, but it is a dependency). No SSH keys or open inbound ports required. Uses IAM for authentication.
aws.keyDetail: No additional cost beyond SSM agent. IAM-based access control with no SSH key management needed. Session logging/recording to S3/CloudWatch. Supports port forwarding and hybrid (on-premises) via SSM agent.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  SSM["SSM Session\nManager"] --> EC2["EC2\nAgent-based"]
  style SSM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: IAP TCP Tunneling
gcp.description: Identity-Aware Proxy tunnels TCP traffic (SSH, RDP, custom ports) to GCP VMs using IAM authentication. No public IPs or open ports needed.
gcp.keyDetail: Free to use. Access via gcloud CLI with local port forwarding. Supports OS Login (no SSH key management). Web-based SSH also available in GCP Console. Context-aware access policies available.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  IAP["IAP TCP\nTunnel"] --> VM["VM\nProxy-based"]
  style IAP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Bastion is a deployed PaaS resource (costs money per hour, per VNet); AWS SSM and GCP IAP are lightweight, software-defined approaches with no dedicated infrastructure. AWS SSM uniquely includes automation/patching capabilities alongside access. GCP IAP uniquely supports arbitrary TCP port forwarding, making it useful for databases and custom services, not just SSH/RDP.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Bastion's Developer SKU is free (single-connection, dev/test only). Basic (~$0.19/hr) is browser-only; Standard (~$0.29/hr) adds native client support. AWS SSM and GCP IAP remain cheaper for production use — no deployed resource, no per-hour cost. AWS SSM and GCP IAP are architecturally distinct — using a zero-trust, identity-based model rather than a network-level bastion (no deployed resource, no per-hour cost). If you're Azure-only, consider alternatives like SSH over Private Endpoint or Entra ID-based SSH.

azure.name: Azure Firewall Manager
azure.description: Central management plane for deploying and configuring Azure Firewall policies across multiple VNets, regions, and subscriptions. Supports hierarchical policies (global + local).
azure.keyDetail: Manages both VWAN secured hubs and standard hub VNets. Supports partner SECaaS integration (Zscaler, Check Point, iBoss). Hierarchical policies allow global base + DevOps-owned local policies.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  FM["Firewall\nManager"] --> P["Central\nPolicy"]
  style FM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Firewall Manager
aws.description: Centrally manages firewall rules across all accounts in an AWS Organization. Manages AWS WAF, Shield Advanced, Security Groups, Network Firewall, and Route 53 DNS Firewall policies.
aws.keyDetail: Auto-applies policies to new accounts/resources for continuous compliance. Requires AWS Organizations. Covers WAF + Shield + SGs + Network Firewall + DNS Firewall — broadest multi-service scope of the three.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  FM["Firewall\nManager"] --> R["SGs + NACLs\n+ WAF + NFW"]
  style FM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Hierarchical Firewall Policies
gcp.description: Firewall policies applied at organization, folder, and project levels. Rules cascade down the hierarchy, providing centralized control with project-level override capability.
gcp.keyDetail: Uses Secure Tags (recommended), network tags, and service accounts for fine-grained rule targeting. Policies span all projects/folders within an org. Built into the GCP fabric — no separate management product to deploy.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  ORG["Org Policy"] --> F["Folder"] --> P["Project\nInherits"]
  style ORG fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS Firewall Manager has the broadest scope — it manages WAF, Shield, Security Groups, Network Firewall, and DNS Firewall from one place. Azure Firewall Manager is focused specifically on Azure Firewall policy hierarchy. GCP's approach is the tightly integrated with the resource hierarchy — hierarchical policies are a native part of the resource hierarchy (org → folder → project), not a separate management tool.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's hierarchical approach is the built into the resource hierarchy — security teams set org-level rules that project owners can't override (unless explicitly delegated). Azure's model is similar but a separate management layer via Firewall Manager rather than baked into the resource hierarchy. AWS's strength is breadth — one tool for all security services.

azure.name: Azure Gateway Load Balancer
azure.description: Transparent bump-in-the-wire insertion of NVAs (firewalls, IDS/IPS) into the traffic path. Chains to Standard LB or VM NIC using VXLAN encapsulation.
azure.keyDetail: Uses VXLAN protocol for traffic encapsulation. Automatic traffic symmetry. Provider/consumer model supports cross-subscription NVA insertion. Chains to public or internal LB frontends.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  T["Traffic"] --> GLB["Gateway LB\nBump-in-Wire"] --> NVA["NVA"]
  style GLB fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
aws.description: Transparent traffic inspection service using GENEVE encapsulation to route traffic through third-party virtual appliances. Maintains flow stickiness for stateful inspection.
aws.keyDetail: Uses GENEVE protocol (port 6081). Integrates with Transit Gateway for cross-VPC/cross-account inspection. ~$0.0125/hr per endpoint + $0.004/GB. AWS's primary service for centralised NVA inspection.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  T["Traffic"] --> GWLB["GWLB\nGeneve Encap"] --> NVA["Appliance"]
  style GWLB fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: No direct equivalent — closest: Cloud NGFW Enterprise or NVA with internal LB
gcp.description: GCP has no native Gateway Load Balancer equivalent for transparent third-party NVA insertion. Cloud NGFW Enterprise (Palo Alto powered) provides managed next-gen firewall as a service but is not a generic NVA insertion mechanism. For third-party NVAs, use internal load balancer with custom routes.
gcp.keyDetail: No bump-in-the-wire transparent insertion like Azure GWLB or AWS GWLB. Cloud NGFW Enterprise: managed firewall endpoints created per VPC/zone, uses firewall policies with Palo Alto inspection engine. For generic NVAs: deploy behind internal TCP/UDP LB with policy-based routes — requires manual route management.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  T["Traffic"] --> NGFW["Cloud NGFW\nEnterprise"] --> I["L7\nInspection"]
  style NGFW fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure (VXLAN) and AWS (GENEVE) both provide native bump-in-the-wire NVA insertion with automatic traffic symmetry — a significant operational benefit. GCP has no equivalent; you must design manual routing and failover for inline NVA inspection. GCP's Packet Mirroring only supports passive inspection (copy, not inline).
features.practitionerNote: GCP does not offer a Gateway Load Balancer equivalent. If you need transparent inline inspection (common for regulated workloads), Azure Gateway LB and AWS GWLB require less custom routing configuration. On GCP, plan for manual UDR management and consider whether Packet Mirroring (passive) meets your compliance requirements. If you're deploying third-party firewalls (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Check Point) inline, Azure and AWS GWLB are both excellent and well-supported by vendors. Azure's chaining model requires fewer configuration steps. GCP does not offer a GWLB equivalent, which affects customers needing transparent NVA insertion—you'll be doing custom routing which is adds operational complexity.

azure.name: NSG + Application Security Groups (ASG)
azure.description: NSGs provide stateful L3/L4 filtering at subnet or NIC level. ASGs add logical grouping of VMs by role, enabling rules like 'allow web-tier to db-tier' without managing IP addresses.
azure.keyDetail: NSGs are free. ASGs allow role-based microsegmentation across subnets without IP management. Rules evaluated in priority order. Max 1000 NSG rules per NSG, 3000 ASGs per subscription.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  NSG["NSG"] --> ASG["ASG\nApp Groups"]
  style NSG fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Security Groups
aws.description: Stateful L3/L4 firewalls applied at instance level. Rules can reference other Security Group IDs as source/destination, enabling tier-based isolation without IP management.
aws.keyDetail: Free to use. Up to 5 SGs per ENI (raisable), 60 inbound + 60 outbound rules per SG. SG-to-SG referencing is the microsegmentation primitive. No subnet-level SG equivalent (use NACLs for subnet filtering).
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  SG["Security\nGroup"] --> REF["SG Reference\nChaining"]
  style SG fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VPC Firewall Policies + Network Tags
gcp.description: Distributed firewall rules applied VPC-wide using network tags or service accounts as targets. Supports hierarchical policies at org, folder, and project level.
gcp.keyDetail: Free for standard rules. IAM-governed Secure Tags (resource-based objects) alongside legacy network tags. Rules can target by tag, service account, or CIDR. Global VPC-wide rules with org-level policy inheritance. IAM-governed Secure Tags available for stronger identity binding.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  FP["Firewall\nPolicies"] --> ST["Secure Tags\nIAM-bound"]
  style FP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure's ASGs and AWS's SG-to-SG references are explicit, named security objects — easier to audit and reference in automation. GCP's network tags are just string labels with no object backing, requiring disciplined tag management. AWS uniquely lacks subnet-level security groups (uses NACLs instead). GCP uniquely supports org-wide hierarchical policies baked into the firewall rules.
features.practitionerNote: For auditable microsegmentation, Azure ASGs and AWS SG references are first-class resources with independent lifecycle management, unlike GCP's legacy network tags which are string labels. GCP's newer IAM-governed Secure Tags (not legacy network tags) close this gap — use those instead of legacy tags in production.

azure.name: Azure DNS Security Policy
azure.description: Active DNS firewall with domain blocking, allow/block/alert rules, and Microsoft Threat Intelligence feeds. GA Nov 2025. Replaces deprecated Defender for DNS (retired Aug 2023).
azure.keyDetail: Active DNS firewall — blocks (not just detects) malicious domains in real time. Enforces at VNet level via DNS Private Resolver. Supports custom domain lists and MS Threat Intel feed. Replaced the older detection-only Defender for DNS.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  DNS["DNS Security\nPolicy"] --> R["Resolution\nFiltering"]
  style DNS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall
aws.description: DNS-layer firewall for VPCs that blocks/allows DNS queries based on managed threat intelligence feeds and custom domain lists. Prevents malware C2 and data exfiltration at the DNS layer.
aws.keyDetail: Active enforcement — can return NXDOMAIN, NODATA, or custom responses for blocked domains. AWS-managed threat lists for malware, botnets, phishing. DNS Firewall Advanced adds DGA and tunneling detection. Managed via Firewall Manager.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  R53["Route 53 DNS\nFirewall"] --> DL["Domain\nLists"]
  style R53 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: DNS Armor + Cloud NGFW FQDN filtering
gcp.description: DNS Armor (GA Jan 2026, Infoblox partnership) provides DNS threat detection and blocking for C2, DGA, tunneling. Cloud NGFW supports FQDN-based filtering rules.
gcp.keyDetail: DNS Armor requires Cloud DNS; FQDN filtering in network firewall policies.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  DA["DNS Armor"] --> TI["Threat\nIntelligence"]
  style DA fill:#EA4335,color:#fff
