features.differences: All three clouds now have active DNS security. AWS Route 53 DNS Firewall (2021) is longest-established with managed threat lists. Azure DNS Security Policy (2025) integrates with Microsoft Threat Intelligence. GCP DNS Armor (2025) is newest, powered by Infoblox.
features.practitionerNote: All three clouds now offer native active DNS-layer security. AWS Route 53 DNS Firewall (2021) is longest-established with managed threat lists and Advanced DGA detection. Azure DNS Security Policy (GA 2025) actively blocks malicious domains via DNS Private Resolver — no longer needs third-party. GCP DNS Armor (GA 2026) adds Infoblox-powered threat intelligence. For multi-cloud consistency, third-party DNS firewalls (Infoblox, Cloudflare Gateway) still make sense if you want a single pane across all three clouds.

azure.name: Azure Firewall Premium (TLS Inspection)
azure.description: Outbound and east-west TLS decryption/re-encryption with IDPS, URL filtering, and web category filtering. Uses customer-provided intermediate CA from Key Vault.
azure.keyDetail: Premium-only feature — not available on Basic or Standard SKUs. Premium: 10 Gbps with TLS + IDPS Deny mode (common production config); 100 Gbps TLS-only or TLS + IDPS Alert-only. Uses customer-provided intermediate CA certificate from Key Vault.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  FW["FW Premium\nOutbound TLS"] --> AG["AppGW\nInbound TLS"]
  style FW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Network Firewall (TLS Inspection)
aws.description: Supports inbound and outbound TLS decryption using Suricata rules for L3-L7 inspection. Certificates managed via AWS Certificate Manager (ACM).
aws.keyDetail: Supports both inbound AND outbound TLS inspection (broader than Azure). Suricata rule engine for custom IDS/IPS signatures. Certificates imported to ACM. Additional cost for TLS-enabled endpoints.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  NFW["Network FW\nTLS Inspect"] --> R["Suricata\nRules"]
  style NFW fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud NGFW Enterprise (TLS Inspection)
gcp.description: Inbound and outbound TLS decryption with L7 inspection including IDPS and URL filtering. Uses Google Certificate Authority Service for dynamic short-lived certificate issuance.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports TLS 1.0-1.3 and wide cipher suite range. Google-managed CA pools via Certificate Authority Service — less operational burden than Azure/AWS manual cert management. Regional deployment requires per-region CA pool setup.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NGFW["Cloud NGFW\nTLS Inspect"] --> P["Policy\nDecrypt"]
  style NGFW fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Firewall Premium only inspects outbound/east-west TLS — inbound requires Application Gateway WAF (two products). AWS Network Firewall and GCP Cloud NGFW both support inbound + outbound in a single product. GCP's use of Certificate Authority Service for dynamic cert issuance reduces operational overhead for certificate management. AWS's Suricata engine gives the most flexibility for custom signatures.
features.practitionerNote: Azure separates outbound TLS inspection (Firewall Premium) from inbound TLS inspection (Application Gateway WAF) (inbound TLS) is a an operational challenge — you need two products to cover both directions. AWS and GCP handle it in one. If you need custom IDS signatures, AWS Suricata is most flexible.

azure.name: Service Tags (in UDRs, NSGs, Azure Firewall)
azure.description: Microsoft-maintained collections of IP prefixes for Azure services (Storage, SQL, AzureCloud, etc.). Usable in NSGs, UDRs, and Azure Firewall rules as logical addresses.
azure.keyDetail: Automatically updated by Microsoft. ~390+ service tags available. Can be used in route tables to steer service traffic through NVAs. Available as downloadable JSON for on-premises firewalls. Cannot create custom service tags.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  ST["Service Tags\nMS-Managed"] --> UDR["UDR\nAuto-Updated"]
  style ST fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Managed Prefix Lists
aws.description: AWS-maintained collections of IP CIDR blocks for specific services (S3, DynamoDB, CloudFront). Usable in security groups, route tables, and Network Firewall rules.
aws.keyDetail: AWS-managed prefix lists for S3, DynamoDB, CloudFront, etc. Also supports customer-managed prefix lists (custom CIDR collections). Shareable via AWS RAM across accounts. Referenced by ID (pl-xxxx) in routes and SGs.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  MPL["Managed\nPrefix Lists"] --> RT["Route Table\nCustomer-Managed"]
  style MPL fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: No direct equivalent
gcp.description: GCP has no managed service tag or prefix list abstraction. Service IP ranges must be manually managed or accessed via published JSON files. VPC Service Controls and Private Google Access provide identity-based alternatives.
gcp.keyDetail: Reusable named IP sets via Address Groups (project or org scoped) for Cloud NGFW and Cloud Armor rules. Also Google Threat Intelligence named lists. Google publishes service IP ranges as JSON but doesn't provide a referenceable object for use in routes or firewall rules.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NA["No Native\nEquivalent"] --> W["Manual\nCIDR Mgmt"]
  style NA fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Service Tags and AWS Prefix Lists are conceptually equivalent — both are auto-updated IP collections usable in routing and security rules. AWS additionally supports customer-managed prefix lists (shareable across accounts). GCP has no equivalent IP-based abstraction; it favors identity and perimeter-based controls over IP-based routing to services.
features.practitionerNote: AWS's customer-managed prefix lists are a differentiating feature — you can create reusable CIDR collections for your own services and share them via RAM. Azure's service tags are read-only (Microsoft-managed). GCP does not offer an equivalent feature, which is a difference noted by network engineers who think in terms of routes and prefixes.

azure.name: Azure DNS
azure.description: Authoritative DNS hosting on Azure's global anycast network. Supports all standard record types. No domain registration — requires external registrar.
azure.keyDetail: 100% SLA. Up to 25,000 record sets per zone. 1,000 zones per subscription (raisable). No integrated health checks — requires Traffic Manager. Supports alias record sets for Azure resources (Public IP, Traffic Manager, Front Door, CDN endpoints). ~$0.50/zone/month.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  DNS["Azure DNS"] --> Z["Zone\nDelegation"]
  style DNS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Amazon Route 53
aws.description: Authoritative DNS hosting with integrated health checks, domain registration, and advanced traffic routing (latency, geo, weighted, failover). 100% SLA.
aws.keyDetail: 100% SLA. 10,000 records per zone (raisable). ALIAS records for apex domains. Built-in health checks with DNS failover. Domain registration included. ~$0.50/zone/month + $0.40/million queries.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  R53["Route 53"] --> HC["Health Checks\n+ Failover"]
  style R53 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Google Cloud DNS
gcp.description: Authoritative DNS hosting on Google's global anycast network. 100% SLA for Premium tier. Full DNSSEC support. No domain registration (use Cloud Domains separately).
gcp.keyDetail: 100% SLA for Cloud DNS. Up to 10,000 records per zone (raisable). Up to ~10K QPS per VPC per zone per project. Cloud DNS routing policies now support integrated health checks for public IPs — supports health-checked routing policies natively (no Traffic Director required). ~$0.20/zone/month + $0.40/million queries.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  CD["Cloud DNS"] --> Z["Anycast\nZones"]
  style CD fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Route 53 has the broadest feature set — integrated health checks, domain registration, and advanced traffic routing in one product. Azure DNS and GCP Cloud DNS are authoritative-only and require separate services for health checks and traffic management. GCP offers the highest raw query capacity (1M QPS/project). Azure DNS lacks domain registration entirely.
features.practitionerNote: Route 53's integrated health checks and ALIAS records simplify active-active and failover DNS patterns. Azure DNS requires Traffic Manager (separate, more expensive) for equivalent functionality. GCP Cloud DNS is the cheapest at $0.20/zone.

azure.name: Azure DNS Private Zones
azure.description: Managed private DNS zones resolvable within linked Azure VNets. Supports auto-registration of VM DNS records. Linkable across subscriptions.
azure.keyDetail: Up to 1,000 private zones per subscription. Auto-registration links VMs to zones automatically. Can link a zone to up to 1,000 VNets. Split-horizon DNS supported (same zone name public + private). ~$0.50/zone (first 25), then $0.10/month.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  PZ["Private Zone"] --> AR["Auto-Reg\nVM Records"]
  style PZ fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Route 53 Private Hosted Zones
aws.description: Managed private DNS zones associated with one or more VPCs for internal name resolution. Supports cross-region and cross-account VPC associations.
aws.keyDetail: Associable with VPCs across regions and accounts. Supports conditional forwarding via Resolver rules. Health checks available for private records. ~$0.50/zone/month for first 25, then $0.10. No auto-registration of instances. Note: Route 53 health probes cannot directly reach private IPs or interface endpoints — use CloudWatch alarms or Lambda-based health signals for private failover.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  PHZ["Private\nHosted Zone"] --> VPC["VPC\nAssociation"]
  style PHZ fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud DNS Private Zones
gcp.description: Managed private DNS zones scoped to specific VPC networks. Supports cross-project VPC binding and DNS peering between VPCs.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports DNS peering (one VPC delegates resolution to another). Cross-project zone binding available. DNSSEC is NOT supported for private zones — public zones only. ~$0.20/zone/month. No auto-registration of VM records.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  PZ["Private Zone"] --> VPC["VPC\nBinding"]
  style PZ fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure uniquely supports auto-registration of VM records into private zones — a significant convenience. AWS Private Hosted Zones support health checks for private records, which Azure and GCP do not. GCP's DNS peering concept is unique — it allows one VPC to delegate name resolution to another VPC's private zones without zone replication.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's auto-registration is a differentiating capability for private DNS — VMs register themselves automatically. AWS and GCP require external automation for the same. For cross-account/project sharing, all three support it but with different mechanics. Budget for the DNS zone sprawl that comes with Private Endpoints.

azure.name: Azure DNS Private Resolver
azure.description: Managed DNS forwarding service with inbound and outbound endpoints. Replaces the need for custom DNS VMs for hybrid resolution between Azure VNets and on-premises networks.
azure.keyDetail: Inbound endpoint: accepts queries from on-prem into Azure. Outbound endpoint: forwards queries from Azure to on-prem/external. Deployed per-VNet. Eliminates need for IaaS DNS forwarders. GA since 2022. ~$0.25/hr per endpoint.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  ON["On-prem"] --> DPR["DNS Private\nResolver"] --> PZ["Private\nZones"]
  style DPR fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Route 53 Resolver (Inbound/Outbound Endpoints)
aws.description: Managed DNS resolver with inbound and outbound endpoints for hybrid DNS resolution between VPCs and on-premises networks. Includes DNS Firewall integration.
aws.keyDetail: Inbound endpoints: on-prem resolves AWS private DNS. Outbound endpoints: VPC forwards queries to on-prem DNS. Conditional forwarding rules per domain. Integrates with DNS Firewall. ~$0.125/hr per endpoint IP + $0.40/million queries.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  ON["On-prem"] --> R53R["Route 53\nResolver"] --> PHZ["Hosted\nZones"]
  style R53R fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud DNS Forwarding / DNS Peering
gcp.description: DNS forwarding zones forward queries for specific domains to on-premises or external DNS via Cloud VPN/Interconnect. DNS peering delegates resolution between VPCs.
gcp.keyDetail: Outbound forwarding via forwarding zones to on-prem. Inbound forwarding requires configuring an inbound server policy (alternative IP). DNS peering: one VPC delegates to another VPC's DNS. No separate 'resolver endpoint' resource — built into Cloud DNS.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  ON["On-prem"] --> FZ["Forwarding\nZones"] --> PZ["Private\nZones"]
  style FZ fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure and AWS both deploy explicit resolver endpoints (dedicated resources with IPs) for hybrid DNS — GCP builds forwarding into the Cloud DNS service itself (no dedicated endpoint resource). AWS Route 53 Resolver is the longest-established, with integrated DNS Firewall and conditional forwarding rules. Azure DNS Private Resolver eliminated the long-standing need for IaaS DNS forwarders in Azure.
features.practitionerNote: Azure DNS Private Resolver was a significant improvement — before its release, Azure customers had to run DNS forwarder VMs for hybrid resolution. All three clouds now have managed solutions. For multi-cloud DNS, chain them: Azure Private Resolver ↔ Route 53 Resolver ↔ GCP forwarding zones via VPN tunnels.

azure.name: Azure Private Link + Private Endpoint
azure.description: Projects a PaaS service into your VNet as a NIC with a private IP. Traffic stays on Azure backbone. Supports 100+ Azure services, partner services, and customer-owned services.
azure.keyDetail: Default limit ~1,000 Private Endpoints per VNet (High Scale Private Endpoint preview supports up to 5,000 per VNet). Requires DNS CNAME chain modification (public FQDN → privatelink.*.FQDN → private IP). Each endpoint gets a NIC in your subnet. ~$0.01/hr per endpoint + $0.01/GB processed.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  VM[VM] --> PE["Private\nEndpoint"] --> SQL[(PaaS)]
  style PE fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS PrivateLink + Interface VPC Endpoint
aws.description: Creates ENIs (Elastic Network Interfaces) in your VPC subnet with private IPs to access AWS services, SaaS, or custom services over the AWS network.
aws.keyDetail: Default 50 interface endpoints per VPC (raisable to 1,000+). Auto-creates private hosted zone entries for DNS resolution. Supports cross-region PrivateLink (2025). ~$0.01/hr per endpoint per AZ + $0.01/GB processed.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  EC2[EC2] --> VE["Interface\nEndpoint"] --> S3[(Service)]
  style VE fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Private Service Connect (PSC)
gcp.description: Consumer-initiated private connectivity to Google APIs, managed services, or producer-published services. Uses forwarding rules and PSC endpoints within VPC subnets.
gcp.keyDetail: Default 50 PSC endpoints per VPC per region (raisable). Consumer endpoint model with explicit connection acceptance. Supports Google APIs, partner services, and custom producer services. Automatic DNS zone creation for Google API endpoints.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  VM[VM] --> PSC["PSC\nEndpoint"] --> SVC[(Service)]
  style PSC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure requires the most DNS work — you must create/manage privatelink.* DNS zones and understand the CNAME chain. AWS creates private hosted zone entries more automatically. GCP PSC is the most abstracted — automatic DNS and a 'connection' model where consumers request and producers accept. All three use NAT to support overlapping address spaces.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Private Endpoint DNS is the a common source of deployment issues — the CNAME chain from publicname → privatelink.publicname → private IP is a common source of configuration errors. Budget significant time for DNS zone strategy (centralised vs per-VNet). AWS is slightly easier for DNS. GCP PSC is the most abstracted model but has the smallest service catalog.

azure.name: Service Endpoints
azure.description: Routes traffic from VNet subnets to Azure PaaS services over the Azure backbone. Services retain public IPs but accept traffic only from enabled subnets.
azure.keyDetail: Free to use. Enabled per-subnet, per-service. Supports Storage, SQL, Cosmos DB, Key Vault, Event Hubs, and more. Service keeps its public IP — adds a VNet rule to the service firewall. No private IP assigned. Being superseded by Private Endpoints.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  SUB["Subnet"] --> SE["Service\nEndpoint"] --> PaaS["Azure\nPaaS"]
  style SE fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Gateway VPC Endpoints
aws.description: Route table entries that direct traffic to S3 or DynamoDB through the AWS backbone. Free, no NAT gateway required. Limited to only two services.
aws.keyDetail: Free to use. Only supports S3 and DynamoDB (all other services require Interface Endpoints/PrivateLink). Adds route table entry pointing to the gateway endpoint. Policy-based access control. No ENI or private IP allocated.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  SUB["Subnet"] --> GWE["Gateway VPC\nEndpoint"] --> S3["S3 /\nDynamoDB"]
  style GWE fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Private Google Access
gcp.description: Subnet-level flag that allows VMs without public IPs to reach Google APIs and services over Google's backbone. Covers all Google APIs.
gcp.keyDetail: Free, per-subnet toggle. VMs without external IPs can reach googleapis.com. Broadest coverage — works with all Google APIs/services. No private IP assigned to services. Traffic still goes to public API endpoints but stays on Google's network.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  SUB["Subnet"] --> PGA["Private Google\nAccess"] --> API["Google\nAPIs"]
  style PGA fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS Gateway VPC Endpoints are the narrowest in scope — only S3 and DynamoDB. Azure Service Endpoints cover many PaaS services but services retain public IPs (just backbone-routed). GCP Private Google Access is the broadest — a single subnet flag covers ALL Google APIs. None of these allocate private IPs to services — that's what Private Link/PrivateLink/PSC are for.
features.practitionerNote: These are all 'v1' approaches being superseded by their Private Link/PrivateLink/PSC equivalents. GCP Private Google Access is still useful as a simple subnet toggle. Azure Service Endpoints are being superseded in favour of Private Endpoints. AWS Gateway Endpoints remain useful for free S3/DynamoDB access in private subnets.

azure.name: Azure Private Link Service
azure.description: Exposes your service (behind a Standard Load Balancer) as a Private Link resource that consumers in other tenants/subscriptions can connect to via Private Endpoints.
azure.keyDetail: Provider deploys Private Link Service behind an internal Standard LB. Consumer creates a Private Endpoint referencing the service's resource ID. Supports overlapping address spaces via NAT. Provider approves/rejects connection requests. Max 1,000 connections per PLS.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  C["Consumer
PE"] --> PLS["Private Link
Service"] --> LB["Provider
LB"]
  style PLS fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS PrivateLink (Endpoint Service)
aws.description: Provider exposes a service behind an NLB as a VPC Endpoint Service. Consumers in other accounts create Interface VPC Endpoints to connect. Native cross-account use case.
aws.keyDetail: Provider creates Endpoint Service behind NLB. Consumer creates Interface Endpoint. Provider controls which AWS principals/accounts can connect. Supports overlapping VPC CIDRs. DNS domain ownership validation required for custom DNS names.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  C["Consumer
Endpoint"] --> PL["PrivateLink
Service"] --> NLB["Provider
NLB"]
  style PL fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Private Service Connect (Producer)
gcp.description: Producer publishes a service attachment backed by an Internal Load Balancer. Consumers in other projects/organizations create PSC endpoints to connect. Explicit connection acceptance model.
gcp.keyDetail: Producer creates a service attachment with accept/reject lists per consumer project. Consumer creates a PSC endpoint with a forwarding rule. Supports overlapping subnets via NAT. Cross-organization support. Max 10 PSC endpoints per service attachment by default (raisable). NAT subnet for PSC; 250 default connections per service attachment (configurable at creation).
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  C["Consumer
Endpoint"] --> PSC["PSC
Producer"] --> ILB["Provider
ILB"]
  style PSC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three are architecturally similar — provider/producer puts a service behind a load balancer, publishes it, and consumers connect via private endpoints with explicit approval. Azure uniquely uses SNAT on the provider side (consumer traffic appears as NAT'd IPs). AWS requires DNS domain ownership validation for custom private DNS names. GCP's accept/reject lists are the most explicit connection control mechanism.
features.practitionerNote: This is the ISV/SaaS architecture pattern. All three clouds support production ISV/SaaS architectures with cross-tenant private connectivity. AWS PrivateLink is the longest-established. GCP PSC producer is newer but well-designed. All three handle overlapping address spaces via NAT — critical for multi-tenant SaaS.

azure.name: privatelink.* DNS Zones
azure.description: Each Azure PaaS service has a corresponding privatelink.* DNS zone (e.g., privatelink.blob.core.windows.net). Public FQDN CNAMEs to privatelink FQDN, which resolves to private IP in the linked VNet.
azure.keyDetail: Requires creating 100+ different privatelink.* zones for full PaaS coverage. CNAME chain: publicname.service.azure.com → publicname.privatelink.service.azure.com → private IP. Central DNS zone management is recommended but complex at scale.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  PE["Private\nEndpoint"] --> PZ["100+ privatelink\nDNS Zones"]
  style PE fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Endpoint Private DNS
aws.description: Interface VPC Endpoints can enable private DNS, which creates a private hosted zone that overrides the public service endpoint DNS within the VPC.
aws.keyDetail: Enable 'Private DNS' on the endpoint to auto-create a private hosted zone. Public FQDN resolves to private endpoint IPs inside the VPC. Simpler than Azure — one toggle per endpoint. Domain ownership validation required for custom DNS names on endpoint services.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  VE["VPC\nEndpoint"] --> DNS["Auto-DNS\nResolution"]
  style VE fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: PSC Automatic DNS
gcp.description: Private Service Connect endpoints for Google APIs automatically create managed private DNS zones. Minimal DNS configuration required — Google handles zone creation and record management.
gcp.keyDetail: PSC for Google APIs auto-creates p.googleapis.com DNS zone. Custom endpoints use service attachment names. Least DNS management overhead of the three. Less customisable — you use Google's managed DNS names, not custom CNAMEs.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  PSC["PSC\nEndpoint"] --> DNS["Auto-DNS\nEntry"]
  style PSC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure requires the most DNS effort — dozens of privatelink.* zones to create and manage, plus understanding the CNAME chain. AWS is simpler — toggle 'Private DNS' on the endpoint and it auto-creates the hosted zone override. GCP is simplest — fully automatic DNS for Google API endpoints. The trade-off is customisability: Azure is most flexible, GCP is least.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's DNS model is the common operational challenge in the entire Private Endpoint story. You need ~100+ privatelink.* zones for full coverage, and getting the DNS forwarding chain right for hybrid scenarios requires careful planning. Use Azure Policy to auto-create DNS zones and records. AWS and GCP require less DNS configuration.

azure.name: Azure Load Balancer (Standard)
azure.description: Regional Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) load balancer with zone-redundant deployment, hash-based distribution, and HA ports for internal workloads.
azure.keyDetail: 100 Gbps+ throughput, 1M+ connections/sec, 1000 backend pool instances. Pricing: ~$0.025/hr + ~$0.01/GB outbound processed.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  LB["Azure LB\nStandard"] --> VM1[VM 1]
  LB --> VM2[VM 2]
  style LB fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Network Load Balancer (NLB)
aws.description: Regional Layer 4 load balancer with ultra-low latency, static IPs per AZ (Elastic IP), and TLS termination. Scales automatically with no pre-warming.
aws.keyDetail: 100 Gbps+ per AZ, millions of RPS. Pricing: ~$0.0225/hr + $0.006/LCU-hr. Charges based on New/Active connections + processed bytes (NLCU model).
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  NLB["Network\nLoad Balancer"] --> T1[Target 1]
  NLB --> T2[Target 2]
  style NLB fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Passthrough / Proxy Network Load Balancer
gcp.description: Regional Layer 4 load balancer built on Google's Maglev system. External uses pass-through architecture preserving client IP; Internal for VPC-internal traffic.
gcp.keyDetail: Hundreds of Gbps, millions of PPS. $0.025/hr for first 5 forwarding rules, then $0.01/hr per additional. Anycast IP available natively.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  LB["Passthrough\nNetwork LB"] --> IG1[Instance 1]
  LB --> IG2[Instance 2]
  style LB fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three auto-scale with no hard throughput cap, but pricing models differ fundamentally. AWS charges per NLCU (a composite metric of connections + bytes), Azure charges per rule + outbound data, and GCP charges per forwarding rule + data. Azure and GCP preserve client IP natively; AWS NLB also preserves source IP. GCP's Maglev backbone is designed for low-latency traffic distribution.
features.practitionerNote: Azure LB Basic SKU is free but not recommended for production. Standard costs per rule + data processed. AWS NLB charges per NLCU which makes costs hard to predict for bursty connection-heavy workloads. GCP pricing is simpler but watch for multiple forwarding rules adding up.

azure.name: Application Gateway (v2)
azure.description: Regional Layer 7 load balancer with built-in WAF (OWASP ruleset), URL path/host routing, SSL offload, cookie-based affinity, and Application Gateway for Containers (AGC) for AKS integration.
azure.keyDetail: Autoscaling capacity units. Pricing: ~$0.228/GW-unit-hr + $0.008/GB processed. WAF adds ~$0.294/GW-unit-hr. Regional only—use Front Door for global.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  AG["App Gateway\nv2"] --> B1[Backend 1]
  AG --> B2[Backend 2]
  style AG fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Application Load Balancer (ALB)
aws.description: Regional Layer 7 load balancer with advanced routing (path, host, header, query string, source IP), Lambda target integration, WebSocket support, and gRPC. WAF attached separately.
aws.keyDetail: Pricing: ~$0.0225/ALB-hr + $0.008/LCU-hr. LCU composite metric includes new connections, active connections, processed bytes, and rule evaluations. Regional only.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  ALB["Application\nLoad Balancer"] --> TG1[Target 1]
  ALB --> TG2[Target 2]
  style ALB fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Regional Application Load Balancer
gcp.description: Regional Layer 7 load balancer with URL maps for advanced routing, native gRPC support, managed SSL certs, and tight GKE/Cloud Run integration. Cloud Armor (WAF) attached separately.
gcp.keyDetail: Pricing: ~$0.025/hr per forwarding rule + $0.008-$0.015/GB. Also available as Global HTTP(S) LB with single anycast IP—unique among all three clouds at L7.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  LB["Regional\nApp LB"] --> NEG1[NEG 1]
  LB --> NEG2[NEG 2]
  style LB fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure AppGW bundles WAF natively (no separate service), while AWS and GCP attach WAF as separate billable services (AWS WAF, Cloud Armor). AWS ALB has the most routing rule types (header, query string, source IP conditions). GCP's regional L7 LB is architecturally identical to its global L7 LB but scoped regionally—meaning upgrade to global is trivial. All three are regional by default; going global requires Front Door (Azure), CloudFront+GA (AWS), or simply switching to the Global variant (GCP).
features.practitionerNote: Azure AppGW v2 capacity-unit pricing can surprise you—autoscaling means costs scale with traffic unpredictably. AWS ALB's LCU model is similarly complex. GCP's L7 LB is the easiest to take from regional to global. If you need WAF, Azure's integrated approach is simplest; AWS WAF and Cloud Armor both charge per rule + request.

azure.name: Azure Front Door (Standard/Premium)
azure.description: Global Layer 7 load balancer, CDN, and WAF in one service. Uses Microsoft's edge network (180+ POPs) for anycast HTTP(S) routing, SSL offload, caching, and Private Link origins.
azure.keyDetail: Standard: ~$0.01/GB transfer + $0.01/10K requests. Premium adds managed WAF rules + Private Link origins. Operates as an edge reverse proxy, not a pass-through anycast LB—it's an edge reverse proxy. Front Door classic retirement planned March 2027 — migrate to Standard/Premium.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  FD["Front Door\nEdge PoPs"] --> R1["Origin\nRegion 1"]
  FD --> R2["Origin\nRegion 2"]
  style FD fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: CloudFront + Global Accelerator
aws.description: CloudFront: global CDN with 600+ POPs, Lambda@Edge compute, and WAF integration for L7 HTTP(S). Global Accelerator: separate L3/L4 (TCP/UDP) global routing over AWS backbone with static anycast IPs.
aws.keyDetail: Two separate services needed for full coverage. CloudFront: $0.085-$0.12/GB. Global Accelerator: ~$0.025/hr + data transfer premium. Neither is a unified global LB like GCP's offering.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  CF[CloudFront] --> GA[Global Accelerator]
  GA --> R1[Region 1]
  GA --> R2[Region 2]
  style CF fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Global External Application Load Balancer
gcp.description: Anycast global Layer 7 load balancer with a single global IP. Traffic enters Google's network at the nearest edge and is routed to the closest healthy backend across all regions. Cloud CDN integrates natively.
gcp.keyDetail: L4 and L7 global load balancers are separate products: External Global Network LB (L4) and Global External Application LB (L7). Single global anycast IP each. Cloud CDN is a checkbox integration on the Application LB. Pricing: per forwarding rule + GiB processed.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  LB["Global App LB\nSingle Anycast IP"] --> R1[Region 1]
  LB --> R2[Region 2]
  style LB fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: GCP's global LB is truly anycast by default—a single IP serves all regions with automatic proximity routing. Azure Front Door and AWS CloudFront are CDN-style edge reverse proxies that terminate connections at edge POPs and re-establish them to origins. AWS uses separate services for L7 (CloudFront) and L4 (Global Accelerator), whereas GCP handles both in a single resource. Azure Front Door combines CDN+WAF+routing but is fundamentally a reverse proxy, not a pass-through anycast LB.
features.practitionerNote: GCP's single-IP global load balancer is architecturally simple — a single anycast IP with automatic proximity routing. Azure Front Door is the closest equivalent but it's a reverse proxy with edge caching, not a true anycast LB. AWS requires stitching CloudFront and Global Accelerator together for similar coverage, which adds configuration overhead.

azure.name: Cross-region Load Balancer
azure.description: Global Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) load balancer that chains regional Standard Load Balancers behind a single anycast global IP. Provides geo-proximity routing with 5-second health probe failover.
azure.keyDetail: Static anycast global IP, client IP preservation, instant failover. L4 only—use Front Door for L7. Links existing regional LBs as backends.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  CLB["Cross-Region\nLB"] --> R1["Region 1"] & R2["Region 2"]
  style CLB fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Global Accelerator
aws.description: Layer 3/4 global traffic accelerator providing two static anycast IPs. Routes TCP/UDP traffic over AWS backbone to optimal regional endpoints (NLB, ALB, EC2, EIP) based on health and proximity.
aws.keyDetail: Not a load balancer per se—it's a traffic accelerator and global failover mechanism. ~$0.025/hr + data transfer premium. Preserves source IP.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  GA["Global\nAccelerator"] --> R1["Region 1"] & R2["Region 2"]
  style GA fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: External Global Network Load Balancer
gcp.description: True global Layer 4 load balancer with a single anycast IP. Routes TCP/UDP traffic to nearest healthy backend across all regions using Google's Maglev backbone.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports both L4 and L7 in the same product family. Single anycast IP, automatic cross-region failover, no separate regional LBs needed.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  GLB["Global\nNetwork LB"] --> R1["Region 1"] & R2["Region 2"]
  style GLB fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure's cross-region LB is explicitly a L4 global tier that chains existing regional LBs—you deploy regional LBs first, then front them with the global tier. AWS Global Accelerator is technically a traffic accelerator, not a load balancer—it routes to endpoints but doesn't directly load-balance across them. GCP's global network LB is the most native: a single product that handles L4 globally without needing separate regional LB deployments.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's approach (regional LBs chained to a global LB) makes sense for existing deployments—just add the global tier. AWS Global Accelerator is powerful but you're paying a premium for traffic traversing AWS backbone. GCP offers the simplest model: one LB, one IP, global by default. For pure L4 cross-region HA, all three work well—choose based on your existing cloud.

azure.name: Azure Traffic Manager
azure.description: DNS-based global traffic routing with six methods: Priority (failover), Performance (latency), Geographic, Weighted, Subnet, and MultiValue. Works with Azure and external endpoints.
azure.keyDetail: DNS-only routing (~60s TTL). Per-query pricing + health check costs. Separate from Azure DNS (hosting). Supports non-Azure endpoints—good for hybrid/multi-cloud.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  TM["Traffic\nManager"] --> P["DNS-based\nRouting"]
  style TM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Amazon Route 53 (Routing Policies)
aws.description: Unified DNS hosting + traffic management in one service. Supports Simple, Weighted, Failover, Latency, Geolocation, Geoproximity, IP-based, and Multi-Value routing policies with built-in health checks.
aws.keyDetail: Most comprehensive routing policy set (8 types including Geoproximity—unique to Route 53). Pricing: per hosted zone + per query + health checks. Also handles domain registration.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  R53["Route 53\nPolicies"] --> P["Weighted /\nLatency / Geo"]
  style R53 fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud DNS (Routing Policies)
gcp.description: Managed DNS with routing policies including weighted round robin (WRR), geolocation, geolocation with geofence, and failover. Failover routing policies with health checks are now GA. Health checks probe from multiple regions.
gcp.keyDetail: Supports WRR, geolocation, geolocation with geofence, and failover routing policies. Health checks probe from multiple regions for failover decisions. For latency-based routing, GCP relies on its Global LB rather than DNS.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  CD["Cloud DNS\nRouting"] --> P["Geo /\nWeighted"]
  style CD fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Route 53 has the broadest feature set, combining DNS hosting + routing + health checks + domain registration in one service. Azure separates DNS hosting (Azure DNS) from traffic routing (Traffic Manager) into two services. GCP's Cloud DNS takes a different approach to routing policies—GCP's philosophy is to handle traffic management at the LB layer (using anycast), not at DNS. This is a fundamental architectural difference: GCP doesn't need DNS-level failover because its global LB handles it inherently.
features.practitionerNote: If you need DNS-based multi-cloud or hybrid routing, Route 53 and Traffic Manager both support external endpoints well. GCP's simpler DNS routing isn't a gap—it's a design choice because their global LB eliminates the need for DNS failover. For multi-cloud specifically, Route 53 or Traffic Manager are better DNS routers since they work across clouds.

azure.name: Azure CDN / Azure Front Door
azure.description: Azure CDN classic profiles (Akamai retired Oct 2023, Edgio/Verizon retired Jan 2025) have been consolidated into Azure Front Door, which combines CDN + global routing + WAF. Front Door uses 180+ Microsoft POPs.
azure.keyDetail: Front Door Standard: ~$0.01/GB + $0.01/10K requests. Classic CDN from Akamai and Edgio (Verizon) are retired — Front Door Standard/Premium is the replacement.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  AFD["Front Door\nCDN"] --> E["Integrated\nWAF + CDN"]
  style AFD fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Amazon CloudFront
aws.description: Largest cloud CDN with 750+ edge locations. Two-tier cache (edge + regional), Lambda@Edge for compute at edge, Origin Shield for origin protection, and integrated WAF/Shield DDoS protection.
aws.keyDetail: 750+ POPs—largest network. Pricing: $0.085-$0.12/GB first 10TB. New flat-rate plans ($15-$1000/mo) bundle CDN+WAF+DDoS+Route53. 1M requests + 100GB free tier.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  CF["CloudFront"] --> E["Largest Edge\nNetwork"]
  style CF fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud CDN
gcp.description: CDN built on Google's global backbone, integrated with the Global HTTP(S) LB. Supports cache modes (CACHE_ALL_STATIC, USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS, FORCE_CACHE_ALL), signed URLs/cookies, and Cloud Armor.
gcp.keyDetail: 100+ edge locations. Pricing: $0.02-$0.20/GiB depending on region and volume. Cache fill charges apply. Tightest LB integration of the three.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  CDN["Cloud CDN"] --> E["LB Checkbox\nSimple Toggle"]
  style CDN fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: AWS CloudFront has the largest edge network (750+ POPs) and longest-established edge compute (Lambda@Edge). Azure is consolidating its CDN story into Front Door, combining CDN+routing+WAF into one product. GCP Cloud CDN is the simplest—it's just a checkbox on the Global LB—but has fewer edge locations (150+). CloudFront's new flat-rate plans are disrupting CDN pricing. Azure's multi-provider CDN (Akamai/Verizon tiers) is unique but being deprecated.
features.practitionerNote: CloudFront has the largest edge network (750+ POPs) and the most established edge compute platform (Lambda@Edge). Azure's move to consolidate CDN into Front Door simplifies the story but means you're buying a reverse proxy + CDN + WAF bundle. GCP Cloud CDN is the easiest to enable (literally a checkbox) but has fewer edge locations than CloudFront or Front Door. For pure CDN workloads at scale, CloudFront's flat-rate plans are compelling.

azure.name: Azure API Management (APIM)
azure.description: Full-lifecycle API management platform with developer portal, policy engine (transform, rate-limit, cache), analytics, versioning, hybrid deployment via Azure Arc/K8s, and SOAP-to-REST conversion.
azure.keyDetail: Tiered pricing: Developer (~$50/mo), Basic, Standard, Premium (~$2,800/mo). Newer v2 tiers: Basic v2 (~$150/mo), Standard v2 (~$700/mo). Self-hosted gateway on K8s for hybrid. Broadest enterprise feature set of the three (dev portal, SOAP-to-REST, hybrid).
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  APIM["APIM\nEnterprise"] --> P["Dev Portal\n+ Policies"]
  style APIM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS API Gateway (REST/HTTP/WebSocket)
aws.description: Serverless API gateway tightly integrated with Lambda, Cognito, and IAM. Supports REST APIs, HTTP APIs (simpler/cheaper), and WebSocket APIs. Usage plans for rate limiting and API keys.
aws.keyDetail: Pay-per-call: $3.50/million REST API calls, $1.00/million HTTP API calls. Free tier: 1M calls/month for 12 months. Designed for serverless architectures with native Lambda integration.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  AG["API Gateway\nServerless"] --> L["Lambda\nIntegration"]
  style AG fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Apigee API Management + API Gateway
gcp.description: Apigee: enterprise API management with monetization, advanced analytics, ML-based anomaly detection, threat protection, and hybrid/multi-cloud deployment. API Gateway: simpler serverless option for Cloud Functions/Run.
gcp.keyDetail: Apigee tiers: Standard/Enterprise/Enterprise Plus—contact sales for pricing. API Gateway is simpler/cheaper for basic use. Apigee has strongest monetization and analytics features.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  AP["Apigee"] --> M["Monetization\n+ Analytics"]
  style AP fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure APIM is the most balanced enterprise platform (developer portal, policies, hybrid). AWS API Gateway is the simplest and cheapest for serverless (pay-per-call, Lambda-native). GCP Apigee is the most feature-rich for API-as-a-product scenarios (monetization, ML analytics) but also the most expensive. AWS API Gateway lacks the developer portal and complex transformation policies that APIM and Apigee offer out of the box.
features.practitionerNote: If you're serverless-first on AWS, API Gateway (HTTP API type) costs $1/million calls — the lowest price point of the three. Azure APIM is purpose-built for enterprise API programs that need developer portals and policy management. Apigee is designed for large-scale API programs and may exceed the requirements of many teams but notable if you're monetizing APIs externally. Azure and GCP support hybrid deployment — APIM via Arc, Apigee via hybrid runtime. AWS API Gateway is cloud-only with no hybrid runtime equivalent.

azure.name: Azure Front Door + Private Link Origins
azure.description: Front Door Premium connects to backend services via Azure Private Link—origin has no public IP. Traffic flows from edge POPs through Microsoft backbone to private endpoints (App Service, ILB, Storage).
azure.keyDetail: Requires Front Door Premium tier. Supported origins: App Service, ILB, Storage, custom Private Link services. Origin approval workflow required. Eliminates origin exposure completely.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  U["Users"] --> AFD["Front Door"] --> PL["Private Link\nOrigin"]
  style AFD fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: CloudFront VPC Origins
aws.description: CloudFront can connect to origins in private VPC subnets (ALB, NLB, EC2) without requiring public IPs. Launched late 2024 as a native feature replacing the old custom-header workaround.
aws.keyDetail: No public connectivity required for origins. Supports cross-account via AWS RAM. Managed secure channel from CloudFront to private VPC resources. No PrivateLink required—it's native.
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  U["Users"] --> CF["CloudFront"] --> VO["VPC\nOrigins"]
  style CF fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Private Service Connect (PSC) + Global LB
gcp.description: GCP uses Private Service Connect to expose services via private endpoints behind Internal LBs. Global LB can route to PSC endpoints, keeping backends fully private with no public exposure.
gcp.keyDetail: PSC provides service-to-service private connectivity. No direct equivalent of Front Door+PL in a single product—requires combining Global LB + PSC + ILB. More complex setup.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  U["Users"] --> GLB["Global LB"] --> PSC["PSC\nOrigin"]
  style GLB fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure offers a tightly integrated approach: Front Door Premium + Private Link origins is a single feature toggle that removes public exposure entirely. AWS CloudFront VPC Origins (2024) achieves the same result natively without needing PrivateLink—CloudFront connects directly to private VPC resources. GCP lacks a direct equivalent product; you must combine Global LB + PSC + Internal LB, which works but requires more configuration and doesn't have the same edge-to-private-backend integration.
features.practitionerNote: This is a key security architecture pattern—edge proxy with zero public origin exposure. Azure and AWS both provide mature first-party support for this pattern. GCP's approach works but requires more manual integration. If you're designing a secure web app architecture, Front Door Premium + Private Link (Azure) and CloudFront VPC Origins (AWS) are the most integrated solutions. GCP teams should look at PSC but expect more moving parts.

azure.name: VNet Flow Logs (Network Watcher)
azure.description: Captures IP flow information at the VNet level (replacing older NSG flow logs). Logs to Azure Storage for analysis via Traffic Analytics, Log Analytics, or SIEM export. Near real-time delivery.
azure.keyDetail: VNet-level granularity (broader than NSG). Free tier: first 5 GB/month, then $0.50/GB. Additional costs for storage + analytics. Near real-time. No sampling.
azure.diagram:
graph LR
  VNet[VNet] --> FL["VNet Flow Logs"] --> SA[(Storage)]
  style FL fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Flow Logs
aws.description: Captures IP traffic at VPC, subnet, or ENI (network interface) level. Logs to CloudWatch Logs or S3. Supports custom log formats and Parquet export. 5-10 minute delivery delay.
aws.keyDetail: Most granular scope (down to ENI level). ~$0.50/GB ingested to CloudWatch. S3 storage at standard rates. Retention configurable up to 10 years (CloudWatch) or unlimited (S3).
aws.diagram:
graph LR
  VPC[VPC] --> FL["VPC Flow Logs"] --> CW[(CloudWatch / S3)]
  style FL fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VPC Flow Logs
gcp.description: Subnet-level flow logging with configurable sampling rate to balance cost vs visibility. Logs to Cloud Logging with export to BigQuery/Storage/Pub/Sub. Near real-time with 5-second aggregation.
gcp.keyDetail: Configurable sampling rate (unique—reduce costs at expense of completeness). Default 30-day retention in Cloud Logging; export to BigQuery/Storage for longer. Per-GB ingestion pricing.
gcp.diagram:
graph LR
  VPC[VPC] --> FL["VPC Flow Logs\nSampling knob"] --> BQ[(BigQuery)]
  style FL fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: All three capture similar flow data (5-tuple, bytes, packets, action) but differ in scope granularity and cost model. AWS is most granular (down to individual ENI), Azure captures at VNet level (simpler but less granular), and GCP uniquely offers configurable sampling to control costs. Azure's flow log feature is included with Network Watcher (pay storage only), AWS charges per GB ingested, and GCP charges per GB with sampling as a cost lever.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's VNet flow logs include a 5 GB/month free tier then $0.50/GB — less expensive than AWS CloudWatch ingestion for most environments. AWS VPC Flow Logs at $0.50/GB ingestion to CloudWatch can get expensive fast for high-traffic environments—use S3 destination and Athena queries instead. GCP's sampling knob is effective for cost control—use 0.5 or lower sampling for large-scale monitoring.

azure.name: Azure Network Watcher
azure.description: Suite of network diagnostic tools: Connection Troubleshoot (live path test), IP Flow Verify (NSG rule check), Next Hop (routing analysis), Effective Security Rules, and interactive topology viewer.
azure.keyDetail: Largest number of diagnostic tools in a single service. Tests both config and live traffic. Includes packet capture, connection monitor, and topology. Integrated in Azure Portal.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  NW["Network\nWatcher"] --> IPF["IP Flow\nVerify"]
  style NW fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Reachability Analyzer
aws.description: Static configuration-based path analysis. Analyzes routing tables, NACLs, security groups, and peering to determine if two resources can communicate—without sending live traffic.
aws.keyDetail: Config-only analysis (no live traffic). Hop-by-hop visualization shows exactly which component blocks traffic. ~$0.10 per analysis. Supports cross-account, cross-VPC.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  RA["Reachability\nAnalyzer"] --> P["Path\nAnalysis"]
  style RA fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Connectivity Tests (Network Intelligence Center)
gcp.description: Hybrid static/live network path verification. Simulates a test packet through config analysis AND optionally sends real traffic to verify actual delivery. Shows which network element blocks traffic.
gcp.keyDetail: Unique dual-mode: config simulation + live data-plane test. Part of Network Intelligence Center. Supports hybrid/on-prem via Interconnect/VPN. Clear diagnostic output.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  CT["Connectivity\nTests"] --> P["Hop-by-Hop\nTrace"]
  style CT fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Network Watcher is a full diagnostic suite (many tools under one umbrella), while AWS and GCP offer more focused path analysis tools. AWS Reachability Analyzer is config-only (no live traffic), making it safe for pre-production validation. GCP Connectivity Tests uniquely offer both config simulation AND live traffic testing. Azure is the only one with a bundled topology viewer and packet capture in the same tool.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Network Watcher is the comprehensive single tool—IP Flow Verify streamlines NSG rule troubleshooting. AWS Reachability Analyzer is great for pre-change validation ('will this route work?') without touching production traffic. GCP's dual-mode testing is a useful capability—use config mode for planning, live mode for debugging. All three are essential diagnostic tools, and practitioners on any cloud should be aware of the equivalents on other platforms.

azure.name: Traffic Analytics (Network Watcher)
azure.description: Managed service that analyzes VNet flow logs to produce visual insights: top talkers, traffic patterns, blocked connections, threat detection, and geographic traffic maps. Built-in dashboards in Azure Portal.
azure.keyDetail: Turnkey visual analytics—enable it and get dashboards immediately. Powered by Log Analytics. Shows traffic maps, allowed/blocked flows, security threats. No query writing needed for basic use.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  TA["Traffic\nAnalytics"] --> V["Visual\nDashboard"]
  style TA fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Flow Logs + Amazon Athena + QuickSight
aws.description: DIY analytics pipeline: Flow Logs → S3 → Athena (SQL queries) → QuickSight (dashboards). Powerful but requires setup. No native turnkey traffic analytics equivalent to Azure's offering.
aws.keyDetail: Athena: serverless SQL on S3, pay per query (~$5/TB scanned). QuickSight: ~$9-$24/user/mo for dashboards. Full flexibility but you build the analytics pipeline yourself.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  FL["Flow Logs"] --> A["Athena +\nQuickSight"]
  style FL fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: VPC Flow Logs + BigQuery + Looker Studio
gcp.description: Similar DIY approach to AWS: export flow logs to BigQuery for SQL analytics, visualize with Looker Studio (formerly Data Studio). Google's scale makes BigQuery fast for large datasets.
gcp.keyDetail: BigQuery: $6.25/TiB queried on-demand (first 1 TiB/month free). Looker Studio: free for basic dashboards. BigQuery's columnar engine excels at large-scale flow log analysis. No turnkey equivalent to Azure Traffic Analytics.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  FL["Flow Logs"] --> BQ["BigQuery +\nLooker"]
  style FL fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Traffic Analytics is the only turnkey, managed traffic visualization service—enable it and get instant dashboards with threat detection, top talkers, and geographic maps. AWS and GCP both require building a DIY pipeline (flow logs → data lake → SQL engine → BI tool). The DIY approach is more flexible (custom queries, cross-service joins) but requires significantly more setup and SQL expertise.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Traffic Analytics gives you visual maps out of the box — a notable productivity feature compared to building custom Athena or BigQuery pipelines. AWS teams end up building Athena queries and QuickSight dashboards from scratch. GCP teams use BigQuery + Looker Studio. Both work well but take hours to set up vs Azure's click-to-enable. Third-party tools like Kentik or Datadog can normalize this across clouds.

azure.name: Connection Monitor (Network Watcher)
azure.description: Continuous end-to-end connectivity monitoring between Azure VMs, on-prem hosts, URLs, and IPs. Measures latency, packet loss, and hop-by-hop topology. Supports TCP, ICMP, and HTTP probes.
azure.keyDetail: No agent needed on destination. Cross-subscription/workspace monitoring. Real-time latency + loss + topology visualization. Supports hybrid via Arc-enabled servers.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  CM["Connection\nMonitor"] --> H["Per-Hop\nLatency"]
  style CM fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Network Flow Monitor
aws.description: Network Flow Monitor (launched 2024): real-time packet loss/latency between EC2 instances — a new service, not a rename of CloudWatch Network Monitor. Internet Monitor: tracks internet-facing app health worldwide. Synthetic Monitor: probes for hybrid connections.
aws.keyDetail: Three separate tools for different scopes. Network Monitor for inter-instance. Internet Monitor for global internet path health. Synthetic for hybrid. Tightly integrated with CloudWatch alarms.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  NFM["Network Flow\nMonitor"] --> M["Flow\nMetrics"]
  style NFM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Uptime Checks (Cloud Monitoring)
gcp.description: Periodic HTTP(S), TCP, or ICMP probes from multiple global locations to endpoints. Focused on availability/uptime rather than path analysis. Integrates with SLO-based alerting.
gcp.keyDetail: Global probe locations for external health checks. No internal path topology or hop-by-hop analysis. Best for SRE uptime/SLO monitoring. Free tier available.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  UC["Uptime\nChecks"] --> A["HTTP/TCP\nProbes"]
  style UC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure Connection Monitor provides the most detailed end-to-end path analysis with hop-by-hop topology for both cloud and hybrid connections. AWS splits this across three tools (Network Monitor, Internet Monitor, Synthetic Monitor) covering different scopes. GCP Uptime Checks are simpler—focused on endpoint availability rather than network path analysis. Azure is the only service that offering topology visualization of the monitored connection path.
features.practitionerNote: Azure Connection Monitor is the comprehensive single tool for 'is my network path healthy?'—it shows every hop, latency per hop, and packet loss. AWS has better internet path visibility via Internet Monitor (unique feature). GCP Uptime Checks focus on endpoint availability rather than network path analysis. For hybrid network monitoring, Azure Connection Monitor with Arc agents is the well-established solution.

azure.name: Network Watcher Packet Capture
azure.description: Captures packets at NIC level on Azure VMs. Stores to local disk or Blob Storage. Supports filters (protocol, port, IP), session limits, and continuous capture (preview) with ring buffer up to 7 days.
azure.keyDetail: Requires Network Watcher VM extension on target VMs. Max 100 concurrent captures per region, 10,000 per subscription. Output as .cap files for Wireshark analysis. Triggered by alerts for automated capture.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  PC["NW Packet\nCapture"] --> A["Ad-hoc\nOn-demand"]
  style PC fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: VPC Traffic Mirroring
aws.description: Mirrors real network traffic from ENIs to monitoring appliances (EC2, NLB targets) for real-time deep packet inspection. No agent required—native to Nitro-based instances.
aws.keyDetail: Full packet copy (not sampled). Granular filter rules. Mirrors to collector instances/NLBs. No agent needed on source. Supports centralized multi-account architectures. Best for IDS/IPS.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  TM["Traffic\nMirroring"] --> C["Continuous\nStream"]
  style TM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Packet Mirroring
gcp.description: Mirrors all packet data from VM NICs to collector instance groups behind an Internal LB. Configurable by subnet, tag, or instance name. Supports ingress, egress, or both.
gcp.keyDetail: Full packet mirror (headers + payload). Policy-based selection (subnet, tag, instance). Collector behind ILB for HA. No agent required. Bandwidth cost for mirrored traffic.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  PM["Packet\nMirroring"] --> C["Continuous\nTo ILB"]
  style PM fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure's approach is VM-agent-based packet capture stored to files, while AWS and GCP mirror live traffic to collector appliances without agents. AWS and GCP are better suited for real-time IDS/IPS integration (traffic mirroring to NVAs). Azure's packet capture is more suited for on-demand troubleshooting. AWS and GCP can capture continuously at scale; Azure's continuous capture is still in preview.
features.practitionerNote: For security teams needing real-time DPI/IDS, AWS Traffic Mirroring and GCP Packet Mirroring are better suited for real-time DPI/IDS—they mirror full traffic to security appliances without agents. Azure's packet capture is great for ad-hoc troubleshooting but doesn't scale the same way for continuous monitoring. If you're running Suricata/Zeek, AWS/GCP traffic mirroring provides the required continuous packet stream.

azure.name: Network Watcher Topology / Network Insights
azure.description: Interactive visual topology of Azure network resources (VNets, subnets, VMs, NSGs, LBs, gateways). 2024 update added cross-region, cross-subscription support. Export as SVG. Health overlays.
azure.keyDetail: Broadest resource type coverage. Cross-region/subscription in single view (2024 update). SVG export for documentation. Integrated with resource health and traffic analytics.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  NT["NW Topology\nView"] --> S["SVG\nExport"]
  style NT fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS Network Manager Topology
aws.description: Centralized topology viewer for VPCs, Transit Gateways, peering, and hybrid connections. Geographic and logical map views. Traffic metrics overlay. Part of AWS Network Manager.
aws.keyDetail: Best for Transit Gateway-centric architectures. Geographic mapping of global network. Multi-account support. Route analysis integration. Third-party tools (CloudWrangler) add more granularity.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  NM["Network\nManager"] --> T["Topology\nMap"]
  style NM fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Network Topology (Network Intelligence Center)
gcp.description: Visualizes VPCs, subnets, instances, LBs, firewall rules, and hybrid connections. Traffic flow overlays with real-time metrics. Part of Network Intelligence Center alongside Connectivity Tests.
gcp.keyDetail: Scoping by project, folder, or organization. Provides real-time traffic overlay and dependency mapping. Hybrid connectivity visualization (Interconnect/VPN). Less mature than Azure/AWS but functional.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  NIC["NIC Network\nTopology"] --> T["Interactive\nGraph"]
  style NIC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure's topology viewer has the broad resource coverage and newest cross-region/subscription capabilities (2024 update). AWS Network Manager excels for Transit Gateway and hybrid network visualization with geographic mapping. GCP's Network Topology is part of the Network Intelligence Center bundle and focuses on traffic flow visualization. All three are portal-based and auto-discover resources.
features.practitionerNote: Azure's 2024 topology update added cross-region/subscription views—cross-region views in a single pane are useful for large environments. AWS Network Manager topology is essential if you're using Transit Gateway. GCP's offering focuses on traffic flow visualization. For production documentation, Azure's SVG export is handy. For multi-cloud visibility, consider third-party tools like Aviatrix or Alkira that show all three clouds in one view.

azure.name: ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
azure.description: Samples network flows over ExpressRoute circuits for traffic analytics. Collects flow logs at 1-minute intervals with 1:4096 packet sampling. Logs to Log Analytics, Event Hubs, or Storage.
azure.keyDetail: Supported on 1Gbps-100Gbps circuits (Provider & Direct). Monitors private + Microsoft peering. Fixed sampling rate (not configurable). Rich schema: IPs, ports, VLANs, ASNs.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  ETC["ER Traffic\nCollector"] --> M["Flow\nMetrics"]
  style ETC fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: Direct Connect CloudWatch Metrics
aws.description: Native CloudWatch integration for Direct Connect monitoring. Tracks connection state (up/down), inbound/outbound bitrate, and packet rates. Alarms and automated actions via CloudWatch.
aws.keyDetail: Metrics at 1-5 minute intervals. No flow-level detail (aggregated bitrate/state only). Strong operational monitoring but lacks the flow-level depth of Azure's Traffic Collector.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  DX["DX\nCloudWatch"] --> M["Connection\nMetrics"]
  style DX fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Cloud Interconnect Monitoring (Operations Suite)
gcp.description: Monitoring via Cloud Operations Suite (Google Cloud Observability). VPC Flow Logs on connected VPCs provide flow-level detail at 5-second aggregation. Dashboard, alerting, and export to BigQuery.
gcp.keyDetail: Flow-level visibility via VPC Flow Logs on interconnected VPCs. Integrates with Security Command Center. No dedicated interconnect-specific flow collector like Azure's.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  IC["Interconnect\nMonitoring"] --> M["Link\nMetrics"]
  style IC fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Azure's ExpressRoute Traffic Collector is the only dedicated private connectivity flow analytics tool—it provides sampled flow logs specifically for ExpressRoute traffic. AWS Direct Connect monitoring is metric-level only (bitrate, state, packet count) without flow-level detail. GCP relies on VPC Flow Logs on connected subnets, which gives flow data but isn't specific to the Interconnect path. Azure provides the most detailed flow-level visibility for private circuit traffic.
features.practitionerNote: If you're troubleshooting 'what traffic is using my ExpressRoute circuit?', Azure's Traffic Collector answers that directly with flow-level data. AWS Direct Connect CloudWatch tells you throughput and health but not what's in the pipe. GCP's approach (VPC Flow Logs) gives you flows but you can't isolate Interconnect-specific traffic easily. For ER/DX heavy environments, Azure's integrated approach simplifies this.

azure.name: Bicep / ARM Templates (+ Terraform)
azure.description: Bicep is Azure's native DSL that compiles to ARM JSON. Modular, readable, with strong type safety and VS Code tooling. ARM templates are the underlying JSON format. Terraform also has full Azure provider support.
azure.keyDetail: Bicep: Azure-only but concise cloud-native IaC. ARM: verbose JSON but fully featured. Both get Day 0 support for new Azure resources. Terraform azurerm provider has near-parity; AzAPI provider enables Day 0 ARM resource support for Terraform.
azure.diagram:
graph TD
  B["Bicep /\nARM"] --> D["Declarative\nNative"]
  style B fill:#0078D4,color:#fff

aws.name: AWS CloudFormation (+ Terraform + CDK)
aws.description: CloudFormation: AWS-native IaC using YAML/JSON with stack management, drift detection, and rollback. CDK: write infrastructure in TypeScript/Python/Java that compiles to CloudFormation. Terraform has full AWS provider.
aws.keyDetail: CloudFormation: AWS-only, automatic dependency resolution, nested stacks. CDK adds type-safe programming language abstractions. Terraform aws provider is the most popular IaC tool for AWS.
aws.diagram:
graph TD
  CFN["CloudFormation\n/ CDK"] --> D["Native +\nL2 Constructs"]
  style CFN fill:#FF9900,color:#fff

gcp.name: Infrastructure Manager (+ Terraform, Pulumi)
gcp.description: Managed Terraform service (Deployment Manager, GA 2024). Legacy Deployment Manager (Terraform-based) is deprecated (EOL 2026). Many teams use Terraform or Pulumi directly.
gcp.keyDetail: Infrastructure Manager GA Aug 2023. Infrastructure Manager (Terraform-based, GA) EOL March 2026.
gcp.diagram:
graph TD
  TF["Terraform /\nInfra Manager"] --> D["HCL\nNative"]
  style TF fill:#EA4335,color:#fff

features.differences: Each cloud has a native IaC tool (Bicep/ARM, CloudFormation, Deployment Manager) but Terraform works across all three and is the most widely used multi-cloud IaC tool. Bicep uses a concise declarative syntax; CloudFormation is the longest-established; Deployment Manager is nearing end-of-life (Google recommends Terraform). For networking specifically, all native tools have full coverage, but Terraform modules let you define consistent networking patterns across clouds.
features.practitionerNote: For single-cloud, use the native tool (Bicep for Azure, CloudFormation for AWS). For multi-cloud or hybrid networking, Terraform is the most widely adopted option—consistent HCL syntax across providers. GCP's Deployment Manager is officially deprecated (EOL March 2026); Google now directs customers to Infrastructure Manager (Terraform-based) or standalone Terraform. If you're managing networking across clouds, Terraform modules + a CI/CD pipeline is the standard pattern. AWS CDK is interesting if your team prefers TypeScript over YAML.
